Use a single FIFO queue with a is incorrect because a single message group ID forces sequential processing of all trades across all symbols creating.

question: A data pipeline processes sensor data from manufacturing equipment. The SQS queue currently has a backlog of 100000 messages and the 8 consumer instances cannot keep up with the incoming message rate. The company needs to clear the backlog and maintain real-time processing going forward. Which scaling approach addresses both the backlog and ongoing throughput?
options: A. Increase the number of consumer instances to 32 by updating the Auto Scaling group desired capacity and configure a target tracking scaling policy based on the ApproximateNumberOfMessagesVisible metric
B. Replace the SQS queue with Amazon Kinesis Data Streams which provides higher throughput for streaming data and can process the backlog faster using parallel shard processing with enhanced fan-out
C. Increase the message batch size from 1 to 10 in the consumer application and enable SQS long polling with a 20 second wait time to reduce the number of API calls and improve processing efficiency
D. Purge the queue to clear the backlog immediately and add more consumer instances to handle the ongoing message rate preventing future backlogs from accumulating in the queue
explanation:
Increase the number is correct because immediately increasing to 32 consumers clears the backlog and target tracking on queue depth automatically adjusts capacity to match.
Replace the SQS is incorrect because migrating from SQS to Kinesis is a significant architectural change that does not address the.
Increase the message is incorrect because while batching and long polling improve efficiency they provide incremental improvements that are insufficient to.
Purge the queue is incorrect because purging the queue loses all 100000 unprocessed sensor data messages which may be critical for.

question: A ticket booking system uses SQS to queue reservation requests. During a popular concert ticket release thousands of duplicate requests are submitted by users refreshing their browsers. The development team wants to prevent duplicate reservations without adding complex deduplication logic to the consumer. Which SQS feature handles message deduplication natively?
options: A. Configure the SQS queue with a delay queue setting of 5 minutes so duplicate submissions within the delay window are merged into a single message by the queue before delivery to consumers
B. Configure the SQS Standard queue with a long message retention period and use the message deduplication ID attribute available on Standard queues to filter duplicates at the queue level
C. Set a short visibility timeout on the SQS queue so that duplicate messages are quickly made visible and consumed by the same consumer which can then discard them based on the message attributes
D. Enable content-based deduplication on an SQS FIFO queue which uses a SHA-256 hash of the message body to automatically reject duplicate messages within the 5-minute deduplication interval
explanation:
Enable content-based deduplication is correct because SQS FIFO queues support content-based deduplication which automatically rejects messages with identical content within a 5-minute window eliminating duplicates.
Configure the SQS queue is incorrect because delay queues postpone message delivery but do not merge or deduplicate messages with identical content.
Configure the SQS Standard is incorrect because SQS Standard queues do not have a deduplication ID feature as message deduplication is only.
Set a short is incorrect because visibility timeout controls how long a message is hidden from other consumers after being received.

question: A video transcoding service uses SQS to queue transcoding jobs. Each job message contains a reference to a large video file in S3 along with transcoding parameters. The company wants to add priority processing where premium customer jobs are transcoded before free-tier customer jobs even if free-tier jobs were submitted first. Which architecture implements priority-based processing?
options: A. Use a single SQS queue and add a priority attribute to each message then configure the consumer to sort messages by priority before processing so that premium jobs are handled first

B. Create two separate SQS queues one for premium jobs and one for free-tier jobs and configure the consumer to always poll the premium queue first and only process free-tier jobs when the premium queue is empty
C. Use an SQS FIFO queue with premium jobs assigned to a message group with a higher numeric ID since FIFO queues process message groups in alphabetical order of their group ID
D. Configure the SQS queue with a shorter delivery delay for premium messages and a longer delay for free-tier messages so premium jobs become available for processing sooner after submission
explanation:
Create two separate is correct because using separate queues for different priorities allows the consumer to implement priority polling where the premium queue is checked.
Use a single is incorrect because SQS does not support message sorting or priority-based delivery and consumers receive messages in no.
Use an SQS is incorrect because FIFO queues process message groups in parallel not in alphabetical order of group IDs and.
Configure the SQS is incorrect because delivery delay is set at the queue level not per-message on SQS Standard queues and.

question: A healthcare company processes patient lab results using an SQS queue. Each message contains sensitive patient data that must be encrypted in transit and at rest. The company security policy requires that encryption keys are managed by the company and key usage is audited. Which SQS encryption configuration meets these requirements?
options: A. Enable SQS server-side encryption using an AWS managed KMS key (aws/sqs) which provides encryption at rest with automatic key rotation and CloudTrail logging of key usage
B. Implement client-side encryption in the producer application using a company-managed encryption key stored in AWS Secrets Manager and decrypt messages in the consumer application before processing
C. Enable SQS server-side encryption using a customer managed KMS key which provides encryption at rest with company-controlled key policies rotation schedules and CloudTrail audit logging

question: A logistics company uses an SQS queue to process shipping label generation requests. The consumer application runs on EC2 instances and each instance processes messages sequentially. question: An e-commerce platform uses DynamoDB for its shopping cart. The cart data must survive any single AZ failure and be readable immediately after writes. question: A content management system stores article data in DynamoDB. Articles have a partition key of article_id and attributes including title body author and published_date. question: A financial services company uses DynamoDB to store account balance data. The application must update account balances atomically when transferring funds between two accounts. question: A social media application uses DynamoDB to store user posts. The table uses user_id as the partition key and post_timestamp as the sort key. question: A web application uses ElastiCache for Redis as a session store. When a Redis node fails all user sessions are lost forcing users to log in again. question: An e-commerce company uses ElastiCache for Redis to cache product catalog data. question: A SaaS application caches tenant-specific configuration data in ElastiCache for Memcached. question: A healthcare application uses ElastiCache for Redis to cache patient lookup results. question: A news website uses ElastiCache for Redis to cache article pages. question: A ride-sharing company uses ElastiCache for Redis to store driver location data that is updated every second. question: A genomics research lab uses EFS to store large sequencing datasets. The company wants the number of tasks to automatically increase when CPU utilization exceeds 70 percent and decrease when it drops below 30 percent. options: A. Deploy the tasks on EC2 launch type instead of Fargate and use EC2 Auto Scaling to add more container instances when CPU exceeds 70 percent which automatically provides capacity for more tasks
B. Configure an Application Auto Scaling step scaling policy with two CloudWatch alarms one at 70 percent for scale-out and one at 30 percent for scale-in attached to the ECS service
C. Configure ECS Service Auto Scaling with a target tracking policy that targets 70 percent average CPU utilization across all tasks in the service to automatically adjust the desired task count
D. Use AWS Lambda to monitor the ECS service CloudWatch CPU metric and call the UpdateService API to increase the desired count when CPU exceeds 70 percent and decrease when it drops below 30 percent
explanation:
Configure ECS Service is correct because ECS Service Auto Scaling with target tracking on CPU utilization automatically adjusts task count to maintain the target percentage.
Deploy the tasks is incorrect because switching from Fargate to EC2 launch type adds infrastructure management overhead and EC2 Auto Scaling.
Configure an Application is incorrect because while step scaling works it requires manual configuration of two separate alarms and step adjustments.
Use AWS Lambda is incorrect because Lambda-based scaling adds custom code to maintain introduces polling latency and duplicates functionality already available.

question: A microservices company runs 15 services on ECS with Fargate. Each service has its own Application Load Balancer costing the company significantly in ALB hourly charges. explanation:

Replace all 15 ALBs with a is incorrect because removing load balancers for external traffic means losing health-check-based routing and direct Fargate IP routing.
Remove all load is incorrect because requiring unique ports for each service complicates client configuration and NLB cannot perform HTTP-level routing.
Replace all 15 ALBs with a is incorrect because Gateway Load Balancer is designed for network security appliances not application traffic routing and deep.

question: A company migrated a monolithic application to containers running on ECS. The application stores uploaded files in the container local filesystem. Use an EBS is incorrect because EBS volumes in ECS with Fargate are ephemeral storage bound to the task lifecycle and.

question: A company runs a sensitive API on ECS with Fargate that processes payment data. question: A company deploys a new version of its ECS service and discovers a critical bug after 20 percent of traffic has shifted to the new version. question: A company runs a machine learning inference service on ECS with Fargate. Each request takes between 100ms and 30 seconds depending on model complexity. question: A company runs batch data processing jobs on ECS with Fargate. Each job processes a file from S3 and writes results back to S3. Jobs take 10-60 minutes to complete. question: A company runs containerized workloads on ECS with EC2 launch type. The container instances frequently run out of memory causing task placement failures. question: A company runs a Kubernetes application on Amazon EKS with worker nodes in a single Availability Zone. A recent AZ outage caused complete application downtime. question: A company runs a microservices application on EKS where some services are CPU-intensive and others are memory-intensive. question: A company runs an EKS cluster that needs to scale the number of pods based on application-specific metrics like queue depth from SQS rather than standard CPU or memory metrics. question: A company runs stateful databases on EKS using StatefulSets with EBS-backed persistent volumes. question: A company wants to run its EKS workloads with the lowest possible cost while maintaining the ability to handle unpredictable traffic spikes. question: A company processes insurance claims through multiple steps: validation, fraud check, adjudication, and payment. Each step calls a different Lambda function. explanation:

Use Amazon EventBridge is incorrect because EventBridge chaining does not maintain workflow state so determining where to resume after a failure.

Use SQS queues is incorrect because while SQS provides loose coupling between steps it does not provide workflow orchestration retry logic.

question: A company uses Step Functions to orchestrate an order fulfillment workflow. One step calls an external shipping carrier API that occasionally takes up to 5 minutes to respond. question: A data pipeline uses Step Functions to process files uploaded to S3. Each file must be split into chunks processed in parallel and then the results merged. The number of chunks varies from 10 to 10000 depending on file size. Which Step Functions feature efficiently handles this variable parallelism?
options: A. Use a Parallel state with branches for each chunk processing step and dynamically add branches at runtime based on the number of chunks determined during the file splitting step

question: A company uses Step Functions to orchestrate a multi-step data processing pipeline that runs daily. question: A company needs to implement a saga pattern for a travel booking system where flight hotel and car rental reservations must all succeed or all be cancelled. question: A company wants to trigger different processing workflows when files are uploaded to different S3 bucket prefixes. question: A company uses EventBridge to process order events from an e-commerce application. question: A company has multiple AWS accounts for development staging and production. question: A company uses EventBridge to coordinate microservices. question: A company implements an event-driven architecture where EventBridge routes events between 20 microservices. The development team needs to test changes to one service without affecting production events or other services. Which approach enables isolated testing of individual services?

question: A company uses SNS to send critical alerts to an operations team via email and SMS. During a recent incident the SNS topic delivery failed because the email subscription was pending confirmation and the SMS delivery exceeded the account spending limit. The company wants to ensure alerts reach the team reliably. Which approach improves alert delivery reliability?
options: A. Create a secondary SNS topic in another Region as a backup and configure the application to publish alerts to both topics simultaneously so that if one Region fails the other still delivers the notifications

B. Replace SNS entirely with Amazon SES for email delivery and Amazon Pinpoint for SMS delivery configuring each service independently with its own retry logic and delivery monitoring for maximum control

C. Confirm all pending email subscriptions immediately and request an SMS spending limit increase through AWS Support then add an SQS subscription as a persistent backup that triggers a Lambda function for alternate delivery

D. Configure SNS delivery retries with the maximum retry count and exponential backoff for both email and SMS subscriptions so that transient failures are automatically retried over a longer delivery window
explanation:
Confirm all pending is correct because confirming subscriptions fixes the pending confirmation issue increasing the SMS spending limit prevents future delivery failures and an SQS-backed.
Create a secondary is incorrect because cross-Region publishing does not solve the subscription confirmation or spending limit issues as the same.
Replace SNS entirely is incorrect because replacing SNS with separate services adds significant complexity without addressing the root causes and both.
Configure SNS delivery is incorrect because retries do not help when the subscription is not confirmed since the message will never.

question: A payment processing company publishes transaction events to an SNS topic. Three Lambda functions subscribe to the topic for fraud detection, analytics, and notification purposes. The fraud detection Lambda occasionally fails causing missed fraud alerts. The company wants to ensure failed deliveries are captured and retried. Which SNS configuration handles delivery failures?
options: A. Configure an SNS delivery status logging feature for the Lambda subscriptions and create a CloudWatch alarm that notifies the operations team when delivery failures are detected in the logs
B. Configure the SNS topic with message filtering that routes fraud-related events to a separate dedicated topic with higher retry settings specifically tuned for the fraud detection workload
C. Add a second subscription for the fraud detection Lambda using a different SNS protocol type so that if one delivery method fails the other provides a redundant path for the same event

D. Configure a dead-letter queue on the SNS subscription for the fraud detection Lambda so that messages that fail delivery after the configured retry attempts are sent to the SQS DLQ for later reprocessing
explanation:
Configure a dead-letter is correct because configuring a dead-letter queue on the SNS subscription captures messages that fail delivery after retry attempts allowing the fraud.
Configure an SNS is incorrect because delivery status logging provides visibility but does not capture or preserve the failed messages for.
Configure the SNS is incorrect because message filtering routes different events to different subscribers and does not improve delivery reliability for.
Add a second is incorrect because adding a duplicate subscription does not guarantee delivery if the Lambda function itself is the.

question: A multi-tenant SaaS application publishes events to a single SNS topic. Each tenant should only receive events related to their own data. Currently all subscribers receive all events and must filter irrelevant messages in their application code. The company wants to reduce processing costs by filtering at the SNS level. Which SNS feature enables per-tenant message filtering?
options: A. Create separate SNS topics for each tenant and modify the publishing application to route events to the appropriate tenant-specific topic based on the tenant ID in the event payload
B. Use SNS FIFO topics with message groups based on tenant ID which ensures messages for each tenant are delivered only to the subscriber group assigned to that tenant through group-based routing

C. Configure SNS message filtering policies on each subscription that match the tenant_id message attribute ensuring each subscriber only receives events where the tenant ID matches their assigned value
D. Configure Lambda event source mapping filters on each subscriber function to discard events from other tenants before the function code executes reducing the cost of processing irrelevant events
explanation:
Configure SNS message is correct because SNS subscription filter policies evaluate message attributes at the SNS level and only deliver messages matching the filter criteria.
Create separate SNS is incorrect because creating separate topics per tenant adds management complexity that grows with tenant count and requires.
Use SNS FIFO is incorrect because SNS FIFO message groups ensure ordering within a group but do not provide per-subscriber filtering.
Configure Lambda event is incorrect because Lambda event source mapping filters work with SQS and other event sources not directly with.

question: A healthcare company sends patient appointment reminders through SNS. Regulations require that all messages be encrypted in transit and at rest and that the company maintains control over the encryption keys. The SNS topic must also enforce HTTPS-only publishing to prevent unencrypted API calls. Which configuration meets all security requirements?
options: A. Enable SNS server-side encryption using an AWS managed KMS key and add a topic policy condition that denies publish actions when aws:SecureTransport is false to enforce HTTPS-only access
B. Enable SNS server-side encryption using a customer managed KMS key and add a topic policy condition that denies publish actions when aws:SecureTransport is false to enforce HTTPS-only access
C. Implement client-side encryption in the publishing application using a KMS key and configure the SNS topic to use TLS 1.3 for all subscriber endpoints to encrypt messages during delivery

D. Enable SNS server-side encryption using the default SNS service key and configure VPC endpoints for SNS to ensure all publishing traffic travels through the AWS private network without internet exposure
explanation:
Enable SNS server-side encryption using a customer is correct because customer managed KMS keys give the company full control over encryption keys satisfying the key.
Enable SNS server-side encryption using an is incorrect because AWS managed KMS keys do not provide company control over key policies rotation or auditing.
Implement client-side encryption is incorrect because client-side encryption adds complexity and TLS version configuration is not an SNS topic-level setting.
Enable SNS server-side encryption using the is incorrect because the default SNS service key does not provide customer key management and VPC endpoints only.

question: A company publishes high-volume sensor data to an SNS topic at a rate of 10000 messages per second. The data must be delivered to a real-time analytics Lambda function and also stored in S3 for historical analysis. The Lambda function can process messages individually but writing each message individually to S3 would create millions of tiny files. Which architecture handles both delivery requirements efficiently?
options: A. Subscribe the Lambda function directly to the SNS topic for real-time processing and add an SQS subscription that a separate Lambda function reads in batches writing accumulated data to S3 in larger consolidated files

B. Route all messages through a Kinesis Data Stream first then subscribe both the Lambda function and a Firehose delivery stream to the Kinesis stream for parallel real-time processing and batched S3 delivery

C. Subscribe the Lambda function directly to the SNS topic for real-time processing and subscribe an Amazon Kinesis Data Firehose delivery stream to aggregate and batch messages before writing to S3 in larger files

D. Subscribe two Lambda functions to the SNS topic one for real-time analytics and one that buffers messages in memory and writes a consolidated file to S3 every 60 seconds to reduce the number of S3 objects
explanation:
Subscribe the Lambda function directly to is correct because SNS can deliver directly to Lambda for real-time processing and Kinesis Data Firehose natively supports SNS.
Subscribe the Lambda function directly to is incorrect because using SQS as an intermediary adds complexity and requires custom batching logic in the Lambda.
Route all messages is incorrect because adding Kinesis Data Streams as an intermediary between SNS and consumers adds an unnecessary additional.
Subscribe two Lambda is incorrect because Lambda functions do not maintain state across invocations so buffering in memory is not possible.

question: A gaming company has game servers in us-east-1 and eu-west-1. Players connect via TCP and experience variable latency due to internet routing. Some players in South America are routed to eu-west-1 despite us-east-1 being closer. The company wants consistent low-latency routing based on actual network performance. Which solution provides optimal player routing?
options: A. Deploy an Application Load Balancer in each Region and use Route 53 geolocation routing to direct South American players to us-east-1 and European players to eu-west-1 based on their geographic location

B. Configure AWS Global Accelerator with endpoints in both Regions which routes players to the nearest Region based on real-time network performance using anycast IP addresses over the AWS global network

C. Deploy Amazon CloudFront with both Regions as origins and configure origin failover to route players to the closest Region using CloudFront edge locations for TCP connection termination and routing
D. Set up VPN connections from each Region to major internet exchange points in South America and Europe to create dedicated network paths that bypass unpredictable internet routing for game traffic
explanation:
Configure AWS Global is correct because Global Accelerator uses anycast IP addresses to route traffic to the nearest AWS edge location then over the AWS.
Deploy an Application is incorrect because geolocation routing is based on geographic location not network performance and does not address the.
Deploy Amazon CloudFront is incorrect because CloudFront is optimized for HTTP/HTTPS content delivery not arbitrary TCP connections used by game servers.
Set up VPN is incorrect because VPN connections to internet exchange points are not an AWS service offering and would require.

question: A financial services company runs a trading API in us-east-1 behind an ALB. The company is deploying a disaster recovery site in us-west-2 and needs a static entry point that can automatically failover between Regions without requiring clients to update their connection endpoints. Clients have firewall rules that only allow specific IP addresses. Which solution provides static IP addresses with automatic failover?
options: A. Configure Route 53 failover routing with Elastic IP addresses associated with NLBs in each Region and provide the Elastic IP addresses to clients for their firewall rules with health check-based failover

B. Deploy AWS Global Accelerator with endpoint groups in both Regions which provides two static anycast IP addresses that clients can whitelist and automatically fails over to the healthy Region based on health checks

C. Use CloudFront with both Regions as origins in an origin group configured for failover and provide the CloudFront distribution domain name to clients to use as their API endpoint with automatic origin failover
D. Configure an NLB with Elastic IP addresses in us-east-1 and use Route 53 health checks to update a Lambda function that changes the NLB target group to point to us-west-2 instances via VPC peering
explanation:
Deploy AWS Global is correct because Global Accelerator provides two static anycast IP addresses that remain constant regardless of which Region is serving traffic and.
Configure Route 53 is incorrect because Lambda-based NLB target changes are complex error-prone and do not provide true cross-Region failover with.
Use CloudFront with is incorrect because CloudFront does not provide static IP addresses that can be whitelisted in client firewalls.
Configure an NLB is incorrect because Route 53 failover changes the DNS resolution from one set of IPs to another meaning.

question: A manufacturing company runs critical production systems on on-premises VMware infrastructure. The company needs a disaster recovery solution that can recover all systems in AWS within 15 minutes of declaring a disaster. The recovery must preserve the exact state of the on-premises servers at the time of failure. Daily backups are insufficient as they lose up to 24 hours of data. Which AWS service provides this recovery capability?
options: A. Use AWS Backup to perform continuous backups of the on-premises servers to S3 and restore them as EC2 instances in AWS when a disaster is declared with recovery points available every 15 minutes
B. Use AWS DataSync to continuously synchronize on-premises server data to EBS volumes in AWS and launch EC2 instances attached to the synchronized EBS volumes when a disaster recovery is initiated
C. Use AWS Application Migration Service to perform a one-time migration of all servers to EC2 instances that remain in a stopped state and start them when a disaster is declared to resume operations
D. Use AWS Elastic Disaster Recovery (DRS) which continuously replicates on-premises server data to AWS and enables launching recovery instances within minutes preserving the most recent server state
explanation:
Use AWS Elastic is correct because AWS Elastic Disaster Recovery provides continuous block-level replication with sub-second RPO and enables launching fully functional recovery instances within.
Use AWS Backup is incorrect because AWS Backup does not support continuous replication of on-premises VMware servers and 15-minute backup intervals.
Use AWS DataSync is incorrect because Application Migration Service performs one-time migrations not continuous replication so the stopped EC2 instances would.
Use AWS Application is incorrect because DataSync is designed for data transfer tasks not continuous server replication and does not replicate.

question: A hospital uses AWS Elastic Disaster Recovery to replicate its on-premises patient records system to AWS. The hospital needs to perform regular disaster recovery drills to validate that the recovery process works without impacting the production replication. The compliance team requires documented proof that DR drills are conducted quarterly. Which approach enables non-disruptive DR testing?
options: A. Perform a full failover to AWS quarterly by launching recovery instances and redirecting production traffic then fail back to on-premises after the test to validate the complete recovery process end to end

B. Use the DRS drill feature to launch test recovery instances in an isolated VPC that do not affect ongoing replication and terminate the test instances after validating the recovery without impacting production

C. Create manual AMIs from the replicated staging volumes quarterly and launch EC2 instances from the AMIs in a test VPC to verify that the server images boot correctly and applications function properly

D. Review the DRS replication status dashboard quarterly to verify that all servers show healthy replication status and document the dashboard screenshots as proof of DR readiness for the compliance team
explanation:
Use the DRS is correct because DRS drill mode launches recovery instances from the current replication state in an isolated environment without interrupting ongoing replication.
Perform a full is incorrect because a full failover disrupts production replication and redirects actual traffic introducing risk to patient care.
Create manual AMIs is incorrect because reviewing replication status only verifies that data is being replicated but does not validate that.
Review the DRS is incorrect because manual AMI creation is a time-consuming process that does not test the actual DRS recovery.

question: A company runs a critical web application on a fleet of EC2 instances in a single Availability Zone. The application experienced downtime during a recent AZ outage. The solutions architect must redesign the architecture to survive an AZ failure with minimal changes to the application code. Which approach best meets this requirement?
options: A. Migrate all EC2 instances to a larger instance type in the same AZ and enable EC2 auto-recovery to automatically restart instances if the underlying hardware fails
B. Create AMIs of the current instances and store them in Amazon S3 with cross-Region replication so that new instances can be launched manually in another AZ during failures
C. Deploy all EC2 instances in a placement group configured as a spread placement and enable detailed monitoring to detect failures faster across the single AZ
D. Deploy EC2 instances across multiple Availability Zones behind an Application Load Balancer and use an Auto Scaling group with a minimum capacity spread across AZs
explanation:
Deploy EC2 instances is correct because distributing instances across multiple AZs behind an ALB ensures traffic is routed only to healthy instances, and the Auto.
Migrate all EC2 is incorrect because auto-recovery only handles host-level failures, not AZ-wide outages.
Create AMIs of is incorrect because AMIs stored in S3 do not enable automatic recovery and require manual intervention, increasing downtime.
Deploy all EC2 is incorrect because a spread placement group within a single AZ does not protect against an AZ-level failure.

question: A media company processes video files uploaded by users. Each processing job takes 30 to 90 minutes and can be interrupted without data loss because progress is checkpointed to Amazon S3 every five minutes. The company wants to reduce compute costs by at least 60 percent. Which EC2 purchasing strategy meets this requirement while maintaining resilience?
options: A. Use On-Demand Instances in a single AZ and enable EC2 auto-recovery so that interrupted jobs are automatically restarted on new hardware within the same AZ
B. Use Spot Instances across multiple instance types and AZs in a Spot Fleet with capacity-optimized allocation, and resume from the last checkpoint on replacement instances
C. Use Reserved Instances with a three-year term across two AZs and configure an Auto Scaling group to maintain a fixed number of instances at all times
D. Use Dedicated Hosts with a one-year reservation in a single AZ and schedule jobs during off-peak hours to take advantage of lower resource contention on the hosts
explanation:
Use Spot Instances is correct because Spot Instances offer up to 90 percent cost savings over On-Demand pricing, and since the workload checkpoints to S3.
Use On-Demand Instances is incorrect because On-Demand Instances do not provide the required 60 percent cost reduction.
Use Reserved Instances is incorrect because Reserved Instances provide only up to 40 percent savings, falling short of the 60 percent.
Use Dedicated Hosts is incorrect because Dedicated Hosts are more expensive than On-Demand and do not reduce costs.

question: A financial services company runs a stateful application on EC2 instances that store session data locally on instance store volumes. Users report data loss whenever an instance is stopped or terminated. The architect must ensure session data persists across instance lifecycle events. Which solution addresses this issue?
options: A. Increase the size of the instance store volumes and configure a RAID 1 mirror across two instance store disks to provide redundancy against data loss on the same instance
B. Enable termination protection on all instances and configure a CloudWatch alarm to notify the team before any instance is stopped so they can manually back up the data
C. Attach additional instance store volumes and run a cron job that copies data to another instance store volume on a different instance in the same placement group every minute
D. Replace instance store volumes with Amazon EBS volumes and configure the Auto Scaling group to use EBS-optimized instances so that data persists through stop and start cycles
explanation:
Replace instance store is correct because EBS volumes persist independently of instance lifecycle, so data is retained when an instance is stopped, started, or terminated.
Increase the size is incorrect because RAID 1 across instance store disks does not protect against data loss when the instance.
Enable termination protection is incorrect because termination protection prevents accidental API-based terminations but does not prevent stops, and manual backup is.
Attach additional instance is incorrect because instance store volumes on other instances are also ephemeral and the approach does not protect.

question: A gaming company needs to deploy a fleet of GPU-accelerated EC2 instances for real-time game rendering. The workload requires low-latency communication between instances and must tolerate the failure of any single host. Which deployment configuration satisfies both the performance and resilience requirements?
options: A. Launch instances in a cluster placement group within a single AZ and rely on EC2 auto-recovery to replace failed instances on different hosts automatically
B. Launch instances in a spread placement group across three AZs with a maximum of seven instances per AZ to ensure each instance runs on distinct underlying hardware
C. Launch instances in a partition placement group with multiple partitions across two AZs so that a host failure affects only one partition while maintaining low-latency networking
D. Launch instances across three AZs without a placement group and use an Elastic Network Adapter with enhanced networking to minimize latency between all instances
explanation:
Launch instances in a partition is correct because partition placement groups distribute instances across logical partitions that map to different hardware racks, so a single host failure.
Launch instances in a cluster is incorrect because a cluster placement group places all instances on closely connected hardware in a single AZ.
Launch instances in a spread is incorrect because spread placement groups limit to seven instances per AZ and do not optimize for low-latency.
Launch instances across is incorrect because without a placement group there is no guarantee of low-latency placement between instances across AZs.

question: An e-commerce company runs its product catalog service on EC2 instances with application configuration stored in user data scripts. When the team updates the configuration, they must terminate and relaunch instances, causing brief outages. The architect needs a way to update configurations without terminating instances. Which approach is most resilient?
options: A. Store configuration in AWS Systems Manager Parameter Store and use the SSM Agent on each instance to poll for parameter changes and apply updates without requiring instance restarts
B. Store configuration in an S3 bucket and manually SSH into each instance to download the latest configuration files whenever a change is made to the bucket
C. Embed all configuration values as environment variables in the AMI and create a new AMI for each configuration change then perform a rolling replacement of all instances
D. Store configuration in the instance metadata service and update the metadata tags on each instance so the application can read the new values by querying the metadata endpoint
explanation:
Store configuration in AWS is correct because Systems Manager Parameter Store provides a centralized configuration store, and the SSM Agent can poll for changes and apply.
Store configuration in an is incorrect because creating new AMIs and replacing instances for every configuration change causes the same downtime issue.
Embed all configuration is incorrect because instance metadata and tags are not designed for application configuration distribution and cannot be dynamically.
Store configuration in the is incorrect because manually connecting via SSH is not automated, is error-prone, and does not scale.

question: A healthcare company must ensure that its EC2 instances running a patient records application are automatically replaced if the underlying host experiences a hardware failure. The application stores all data in Amazon RDS and has no local state. Which configuration provides automatic instance recovery with the least operational overhead?
options: A. Write a Lambda function that runs every minute to check the status of each instance using the DescribeInstanceStatus API and terminates and relaunches any instance with a failed check
B. Configure an Auto Scaling group with a minimum and maximum capacity of one instance per group and rely on the default health check to replace unhealthy instances automatically
C. Create a CloudWatch alarm that monitors the StatusCheckFailed_System metric and configure the alarm action to recover the instance, maintaining the same private IP and Elastic IP address
D. Enable detailed monitoring on each instance and configure a CloudWatch dashboard that displays hardware status so the operations team can manually restart failed instances promptly
explanation:
Create a CloudWatch is correct because EC2 auto-recovery via CloudWatch alarm actions on StatusCheckFailed_System automatically moves the instance to healthy hardware while preserving network configuration.
Write a Lambda is incorrect because writing and maintaining a custom Lambda function adds significant operational complexity compared to native recovery.
Configure an Auto is incorrect because an ASG would launch a new instance with a different instance ID and potentially a.
Enable detailed monitoring is incorrect because manual intervention does not meet the requirement for automatic replacement.

question: A logistics company runs a monolithic application on a single large EC2 instance. During peak shipping seasons, the instance reaches 95 percent CPU utilization and response times degrade. The company wants to improve resilience and handle peak loads. Which approach should the solutions architect recommend?
options: A. Vertically scale by changing to the next larger instance type during peak periods and schedule the resize using AWS Instance Scheduler to automate the stop, resize, and start process
B. Refactor the application into microservices deployed on multiple smaller EC2 instances behind an ALB with Auto Scaling to horizontally scale based on CPU utilization thresholds
C. Enable EC2 burstable performance by switching to a T3 instance type so the application can use burst credits to handle temporary CPU spikes during the peak shipping season
D. Add a second identical EC2 instance and use Route 53 weighted routing to split traffic evenly between the two instances while manually adding more instances during peak demand
explanation:
Refactor the application is correct because refactoring into microservices with horizontal scaling behind an ALB provides both resilience through redundancy and the ability to scale.
Vertically scale by is incorrect because vertical scaling requires downtime during the resize and has upper limits on instance size.
Enable EC2 burstable is incorrect because T3 burst credits are designed for short temporary spikes, not sustained peak-season loads lasting days.
Add a second is incorrect because Route 53 weighted routing does not perform health checks at the application layer as effectively.

question: A SaaS provider runs Windows-based EC2 instances that require persistent storage for application binaries and log files. The team noticed that when instances are replaced by Auto Scaling, the log files from terminated instances are permanently lost. Which storage solution preserves log files while supporting the Windows instances?
options: A. Increase the root EBS volume size on each instance and configure the Auto Scaling group to retain EBS volumes on termination so logs can be manually attached to new instances
B. Create an S3 bucket and install a third-party file system driver on each Windows instance that mounts the S3 bucket as a local drive for writing log files directly to object storage
C. Configure Amazon FSx for Windows File Server as a shared file system and mount it on all instances so that log files written to the shared path persist when instances are terminated
D. Attach an additional instance store volume to each Windows instance and configure a scheduled task that copies log files to another instance store volume on a backup instance hourly
explanation:
Configure Amazon FSx is correct because Amazon FSx for Windows File Server provides a fully managed Windows-compatible shared file system using SMB protocol, and log.
Increase the root is incorrect because retaining detached EBS volumes requires manual reattachment and does not automatically make logs available to.
Create an S3 is incorrect because S3 is object storage and mounting it as a file system with third-party drivers adds.
Attach an additional is incorrect because instance store volumes are ephemeral and data is lost when instances are terminated.

question: A research institution runs computational workloads on EC2 instances that process large datasets. Each job takes approximately four hours and the results are written to S3 upon completion. The institution wants to protect against instance failures mid-job without restarting the entire computation. Which strategy minimizes wasted compute time?
options: A. Implement application-level checkpointing that saves intermediate results to S3 at regular intervals and resumes from the last checkpoint when a replacement instance starts the job
B. Use larger EC2 instances to complete jobs faster, reducing the window of vulnerability to instance failure, and enable detailed monitoring to detect failures within one minute
C. Run jobs on Dedicated Hosts to avoid the noisy neighbor problem and use EC2 auto-recovery to ensure the job continues on the same host after a brief hardware interruption
D. Deploy two identical EC2 instances that process the same job simultaneously so that if one instance fails the other instance can still complete the job and write results to S3
explanation:
Implement application-level checkpointing is correct because application-level checkpointing to S3 at regular intervals means that if an instance fails, a replacement instance can resume from.
Use larger EC2 is incorrect because running duplicate jobs doubles the cost and wastes resources when both instances complete successfully.
Run jobs on is incorrect because Dedicated Hosts do not prevent all hardware failures, and auto-recovery restarts the instance but does.
Deploy two identical is incorrect because using larger instances only reduces job duration but does not prevent data loss or wasted.

question: A company operates a legacy application that requires a fixed private IP address because downstream systems connect to it using hardcoded IP addresses. The application runs on a single EC2 instance and the company needs to ensure high availability. Which solution maintains the fixed IP requirement while improving resilience?
options: A. Deploy two instances in different AZs each with a static private IP and use Route 53 health checks with failover routing to direct traffic to the healthy instance automatically
B. Place the instance behind a Network Load Balancer with a static IP address and configure the downstream systems to connect to the NLB IP instead of the instance private IP directly
C. Create an ENI with a specific private IP address and write a Lambda function triggered by instance state changes to detach the ENI from a failed instance and attach it to a standby instance
D. Assign an Elastic IP to the instance and create a CloudWatch alarm with a recover action so that the instance retains its network configuration including the Elastic IP after host failure
explanation:
Assign an Elastic is correct because EC2 auto-recovery via CloudWatch alarm preserves the instance ID, private IP addresses, Elastic IP addresses, and all instance metadata.
Deploy two instances is incorrect because the downstream systems use hardcoded IP addresses and Route 53 provides DNS-based failover which requires.
Place the instance is incorrect because it requires changing the hardcoded IP addresses on downstream systems to point to the NLB.
Create an ENI is incorrect because moving ENIs between AZs is not supported, and this approach adds complexity with custom Lambda.

question: A company runs a batch processing system where EC2 instances pull jobs from an SQS queue. Occasionally instances fail silently and jobs become stuck in-flight. The visibility timeout is set to 15 minutes but some jobs take up to 30 minutes. Which change prevents jobs from being processed by multiple instances simultaneously while handling instance failures?
options: A. Increase the visibility timeout to 45 minutes and configure a dead-letter queue with a maximum receive count of three to catch jobs that repeatedly fail after multiple processing attempts
B. Set the visibility timeout to 60 minutes and enable long polling on the queue with a 20-second wait time so that instances only receive messages when jobs are available for processing
C. Switch from standard SQS queues to FIFO queues to guarantee exactly-once processing and set the visibility timeout to 15 minutes since FIFO queues prevent duplicate delivery entirely

D. Have the processing application call ChangeMessageVisibility to extend the timeout periodically while the job is still running and configure a dead-letter queue for jobs that exceed maximum receives
explanation:
Have the processing is correct because calling ChangeMessageVisibility periodically while a job is in progress dynamically extends the timeout, preventing the message from becoming visible.
Increase the visibility is incorrect because A dead-letter queue catches persistently failing jobs.
Set the visibility is incorrect because FIFO queues have throughput limitations and still use visibility timeouts for in-flight message handling.
Switch from standard is incorrect because if an instance fails, it stops extending the timeout and the message becomes visible again for reprocessing.

question: A retail company runs an RDS MySQL database that serves its online storefront. During a recent AZ outage, the database was unavailable for 45 minutes while the team manually restored from a snapshot. The company requires the database to recover automatically within minutes. Which configuration meets this requirement?
options: A. Create an RDS read replica in another AZ and configure the application to automatically promote the read replica to a standalone primary instance when the original primary becomes unavailable
B. Enable automated backups with a one-hour backup window and configure a Lambda function to restore the most recent automated backup to a new RDS instance if a health check fails
C. Enable RDS Multi-AZ deployment so that a synchronous standby replica in another AZ is automatically promoted when the primary instance fails, with failover completing in one to two minutes
D. Deploy a second independent RDS instance in another AZ with bi-directional replication using MySQL native replication and manually switch the application connection string during failures
explanation:
Enable RDS Multi-AZ is correct because RDS Multi-AZ maintains a synchronous standby replica that is automatically promoted during a failure without manual intervention, typically completing.
Create an RDS is incorrect because read replicas use asynchronous replication and promotion is not automatic, requiring manual intervention or custom.
Enable automated backups is incorrect because restoring from a backup takes significantly longer than a few minutes depending on database size.
Deploy a second is incorrect because it requires manual intervention to switch connection strings, does not meet the automatic recovery requirement.

question: A SaaS company uses Amazon RDS PostgreSQL for its multi-tenant application. The database experiences heavy read traffic during business hours, causing slow query responses. Write traffic is relatively light. The company needs to improve read performance without impacting write availability. Which solution is most appropriate?
options: A. Create multiple RDS read replicas and configure the application to direct read queries to the replicas using a reader endpoint while sending all write operations to the primary instance
B. Upgrade the RDS instance to a larger instance class with more CPU and memory so that the single instance can handle both the read and write workloads during peak business hours
C. Enable RDS Multi-AZ deployment to create a standby instance that can serve read traffic during business hours and handle failover if the primary instance experiences an outage
D. Migrate the database to Amazon Redshift which is optimized for read-heavy analytical workloads and can handle concurrent queries more efficiently than RDS PostgreSQL
explanation:
Create multiple RDS is correct because RDS read replicas offload read traffic from the primary instance, and applications can be configured to send read queries.
Upgrade the RDS is incorrect because the Multi-AZ standby instance cannot serve read traffic as it is only used for failover.
Enable RDS Multi-AZ is incorrect because vertical scaling has limits and does not provide the same level of read scaling as.
Migrate the database is incorrect because Redshift is designed for analytical and data warehouse workloads, not transactional OLTP workloads like a.

question: A company stores customer order data in an RDS MySQL database. A developer accidentally deleted a critical table during a routine maintenance window. The company must be able to restore the database to any point within the last seven days. Which RDS feature enables this recovery?
options: A. Create manual DB snapshots every hour using a scheduled Lambda function and restore the most recent snapshot taken before the deletion to a new RDS instance for data recovery
B. Enable automated backups with a retention period of seven days and use point-in-time recovery to restore the database to a new instance set to the timestamp just before the deletion occurred
C. Enable the RDS event notification service to capture all DDL changes and use the event log to reconstruct the deleted table by replaying the creation and insert statements on a new instance
D. Configure cross-Region read replicas that lag behind the primary by several hours so that deleted data can be retrieved from the replica before the deletion event replicates to it
explanation:
Enable automated backups is correct because RDS automated backups combined with transaction log archival enable point-in-time recovery to any second within the retention period, allowing.
Create manual DB is incorrect because hourly snapshots could result in up to an hour of data loss and require significant.
Enable the RDS is incorrect because RDS event notifications track operational events like failovers and maintenance, not individual DDL or DML.
Configure cross-Region read is incorrect because cross-Region replicas do not provide configurable replication lag and typically replicate changes within seconds, making.

question: A global media company needs to provide low-latency database reads to users in North America, Europe, and Asia Pacific. The primary RDS PostgreSQL database is in us-east-1. Write traffic occurs only from the North American region. Which architecture minimizes read latency for all regions?
options: A. Enable RDS Multi-AZ in us-east-1 and use Amazon CloudFront with a custom origin pointing to the RDS endpoint to cache database query results at edge locations in all three regions
B. Deploy independent RDS instances in each region with bi-directional replication using AWS Database Migration Service running continuously to synchronize data between all three databases
C. Create cross-Region read replicas in eu-west-1 and ap-southeast-1 and configure each regional application tier to read from the local replica while directing all writes to the primary in us-east-1
D. Migrate the database to Amazon DynamoDB global tables which automatically replicate data across all three regions and provide single-digit millisecond read latency from any region
explanation:

question: A company runs an RDS Oracle database with a 2 TB dataset. question: A company is migrating from a self-managed MySQL database on EC2 to Amazon RDS. The application requires encryption of data at rest for compliance. question: A financial application uses an RDS SQL Server database with Multi-AZ enabled. The operations team wants to be notified immediately when a failover occurs so they can verify application connectivity. Which monitoring approach provides the fastest notification?

question: A company uses Amazon Aurora MySQL for a customer-facing application. The database currently has one writer instance. question: A company uses Amazon RDS PostgreSQL and needs to run complex analytical queries that take 10 to 15 minutes to complete. question: A company operates an Amazon Aurora PostgreSQL cluster with one writer and two reader instances. The application connection string is hardcoded to the writer endpoint. question: A company must comply with a regulation that requires database backups to be stored in a separate AWS Region from the production database. The production RDS MySQL database runs in us-west-2. Which approach meets this compliance requirement with the least operational effort?

question: A ride-sharing company runs its dispatch service on EC2 instances in an Auto Scaling group. question: An e-commerce platform uses an Auto Scaling group with a target tracking scaling policy set to maintain average CPU at 50 percent. question: A company runs a web application in an Auto Scaling group across three AZs. After a deployment, one AZ has instances running old code while the other two AZs have updated code. question: A company uses an Auto Scaling group with a minimum of 2 and maximum of 10 instances across two AZs. question: A machine learning company runs GPU inference workloads on EC2 P3 instances in an Auto Scaling group. question: A company deploys a new application version using a rolling update in its Auto Scaling group. question: A SaaS company wants its Auto Scaling group to scale based on the number of requests queued in an SQS queue rather than CPU utilization. Each instance can process 100 messages per minute. Which scaling configuration is most appropriate?

question: A company uses predictive scaling for its Auto Scaling group to handle daily traffic patterns. The group currently uses On-Demand Instances exclusively. question: A company needs to perform initialization tasks on new EC2 instances launched by an Auto Scaling group, including registering with a configuration management system and downloading application artifacts. question: A company hosts its primary website in us-east-1 and a disaster recovery copy in eu-west-1. During normal operations, all traffic should go to us-east-1. question: A global retail company wants to route users to the AWS Region that provides the lowest network latency for their location. The application runs in us-east-1, eu-central-1, and ap-northeast-1 with identical deployments. Which Route 53 routing configuration achieves this goal?

question: A company has a three-tier application with web, application, and database layers. The web layer is behind an ALB. explanation:

question: A company migrates its DNS from an on-premises provider to Amazon Route 53. The company has 500 DNS records and needs to minimize the risk of DNS resolution failures during the migration. Which migration strategy is most resilient?

explanation:
Create all records is correct because creating all records in Route 53 first ensures they are ready before the name server switch.

question: A media streaming company uses Route 53 with health checks to route traffic between two ALBs in different Regions. The health checks occasionally report false failures due to transient network issues, causing unnecessary failovers. Which configuration reduces false failover events?

question: A company operates a multi-Region active-active architecture for its customer portal. Users in different continents should be directed to the nearest healthy Region. If both Regions are healthy, traffic should be split based on user location. Which Route 53 configuration achieves this?
options: A. Use simple routing with two A records pointing to both Regional ALBs and let the client randomly select one of the returned IP addresses for each DNS query regardless of geographic location or health status

question: A company runs a microservices application where different URL paths map to different backend services. The application requires TLS termination at the load balancer and path-based routing to direct traffic to the appropriate target groups. Which ELB type and configuration meets these requirements?
options: A. Deploy an Application Load Balancer with HTTPS listeners and configure listener rules with path-based conditions to route requests to the appropriate target group for each microservice

explanation:

question: A financial trading application requires ultra-low latency TCP connections to backend servers with static IP addresses that clients can add to their firewall allow lists. question: A company uses an ALB for its web application. The application stores session state on each instance. Which ALB feature resolves this issue?
options: A. Enable cross-zone load balancing on the ALB to ensure traffic is distributed evenly across all AZs, which naturally reduces the chance of a user hitting different instances across multiple requests
B. Configure the ALB to use least outstanding requests routing algorithm instead of round-robin so that each user connection is sent to the instance with the fewest active connections at that moment
C. Enable sticky sessions on the target group using application-based cookies so that the ALB routes subsequent requests from the same user to the same backend instance for session consistency

explanation:
Enable sticky sessions is correct because sticky sessions bind a user to a specific backend instance using cookies, ensuring all requests in a session go.
Enable cross-zone load is incorrect because cross-zone load balancing distributes traffic evenly across AZs but does not ensure a specific user.

Enable connection draining is incorrect because connection draining helps during deregistration but does not address session consistency during normal operation when.

question: A company runs a WebSocket-based real-time collaboration application. Users maintain long-lived connections to backend instances for document editing. Implement client-side reconnection is incorrect because while client-side reconnection is a good practice, it does not prevent the disruption itself and.

question: A company uses an ALB with health checks configured to verify instance health every 30 seconds. The application occasionally takes 60 seconds to fully initialize after launch. Newly launched instances are being marked unhealthy and terminated by the Auto Scaling group before they finish starting up. Which adjustment resolves this issue?
options: A. Increase the healthy threshold from 2 to 10 consecutive successful health checks so the instance must prove stability over a longer period before being considered healthy by the load balancer

question: A company operates a multi-tenant SaaS platform where each tenant has a unique subdomain such as tenant1.example.com. All tenants share the same set of backend servers. The architect needs to route requests based on the Host header to different target groups for premium tenants while serving standard tenants from a shared pool. Which approach achieves this?
options: A. Configure ALB listener rules with host-based routing conditions that match premium tenant subdomains and forward to dedicated target groups, with a default rule directing remaining traffic to the shared pool

B. Create separate ALBs for each premium tenant with dedicated target groups and use Route 53 alias records to point each premium subdomain to its specific ALB while standard tenants share a common ALB

C. Deploy a Network Load Balancer with TLS listeners and use SNI to identify the tenant subdomain, then route traffic to different target groups based on the SSL certificate presented by the client connection

D. Use an API Gateway with custom domain names for each premium tenant and configure separate stage deployments that route to different backend integrations while standard tenants use a shared API stage
explanation:

Use an API is incorrect because API Gateway adds unnecessary complexity and cost for simple host-based routing that an ALB handles.

question: A healthcare application behind an ALB must ensure that all traffic between clients and the backend instances is encrypted end-to-end. The ALB currently terminates TLS and communicates with instances over HTTP. Which change establishes end-to-end encryption?
options: A. Switch from an ALB to a Classic Load Balancer in TCP mode so that TLS connections pass through directly to the backend instances without any termination or re-encryption at the load balancer layer
B. Enable the ALB access logs and store them in an encrypted S3 bucket to ensure all traffic metadata is encrypted at rest, which satisfies the end-to-end encryption compliance requirement fully
C. Configure the ALB target group to use HTTPS protocol and install TLS certificates on the backend instances so the ALB re-encrypts traffic before forwarding it to the instances over port 443

Switch from an is incorrect because switching to a Classic Load Balancer loses ALB features like path-based routing, and TCP pass-through.
Enable the ALB is incorrect because encrypting access logs at rest does not encrypt traffic in transit between the ALB and.

question: A company runs an application behind an ALB that serves both API and web traffic. API requests go to /api/* and must return JSON error responses, while web requests serve HTML pages. When a backend instance is unavailable, the ALB returns a generic 502 error. The architect wants custom error responses for each traffic type. Which solution achieves this?
options: A. Configure ALB listener rules with fixed-response actions that return appropriate JSON or HTML error messages when no healthy targets are available, using separate rules for /api/* and all other paths

B. Create a custom error page in Amazon S3 and configure the ALB to redirect all 502 errors to the S3 page, then use JavaScript on the page to detect whether the original request was for API or web content

C. Deploy a Lambda function as an ALB target that catches 502 errors from the primary target group and returns the appropriate response format based on the request path before forwarding to the client

D. Configure the ALB access logs to detect 502 errors and trigger a CloudWatch alarm that invokes a Lambda function to update the ALB listener rules dynamically with custom error responses on each failure
explanation:
Configure ALB listener is correct because ALB fixed-response actions can return custom status codes and body content including JSON or HTML, and separate listener rules.
Create a custom is incorrect because ALBs cannot redirect based on specific error codes, and using JavaScript to detect the original.
Deploy a Lambda is incorrect because a Lambda function target would need to proxy all traffic, not just errors, adding latency.
Configure the ALB is incorrect because access log processing and dynamic rule updates are asynchronous and cannot provide immediate error responses.

question: A company exposes internal microservices to other teams within the same VPC using an internal ALB. The security team requires that only specific security groups can access the internal ALB. Recently, they discovered that any instance in the VPC could reach the ALB. Which approach restricts access to authorized services only?
options: A. Move the internal ALB to a dedicated VPC and create VPC peering connections only with the VPCs containing authorized services to physically isolate the load balancer from unauthorized access
B. Configure network ACLs on the ALB subnets to allow traffic only from the specific CIDR ranges of authorized service subnets and deny all other inbound traffic at the network ACL layer
C. Configure the ALB security group inbound rules to allow traffic only from the specific security groups of authorized services, denying all other inbound traffic to the internal ALB listener ports

explanation:

question: A media company stores original video files in S3. Editors frequently need to recover previous versions of files that were accidentally overwritten. Currently, overwritten files cannot be retrieved. Which S3 feature enables recovery of previous file versions?
options: A. Enable S3 Object Lock in governance mode on the bucket to prevent any object from being overwritten, requiring editors to submit a request to unlock the object before uploading a new version

question: A healthcare organization must store patient records in S3 for seven years to comply with regulatory requirements. The records must not be deleted or modified by any user, including the root account, during the retention period. Which S3 configuration enforces this compliance requirement?
options: A. Enable S3 versioning and create a bucket policy that denies s3:DeleteObject and s3:PutObject actions for all principals, with a condition that checks whether the object age is less than seven years

question: A company stores critical business data in a single S3 bucket in us-east-1. The compliance team requires that a copy of all data be maintained in a different Region for disaster recovery. Changes to data in the primary bucket must be reflected in the secondary bucket within 15 minutes. Which solution meets these requirements?
options: A. Create a Lambda function triggered by S3 event notifications that copies each new or modified object to a bucket in us-west-2 using the S3 CopyObject API within the function execution timeout period

Configure S3 Same-Region is incorrect because adding an intermediate same-Region replication step increases latency and complexity without any benefit over direct.

question: A news organization stores millions of article images in S3. question: A software company distributes build artifacts from an S3 bucket to development teams. A developer accidentally deleted a critical artifact file and the company has no backup. The bucket does not have versioning enabled. Which preventive measure should the architect implement to protect against accidental deletions going forward?
options: A. Create a bucket policy that denies all s3:DeleteObject requests from IAM users and allows deletions only through a specific IAM role that requires approval from two administrators before use

question: A data analytics company runs queries against large CSV files stored in S3. Currently, the application downloads entire files to EC2 instances for processing, which is slow for files over 10 GB. The queries typically need only a subset of columns and rows. Which S3 feature reduces data transfer and speeds up query processing?

question: A company uses S3 to store application logs from hundreds of EC2 instances. Each instance writes log files to the same S3 prefix every minute, creating thousands of PUT requests. The company reports that some log files are being lost. Which S3 behavior most likely explains this issue and how should it be resolved?
options: A. Multiple instances are writing files with identical keys at the same millisecond and the last writer wins, overwriting earlier files. Add instance ID and timestamp to each object key to ensure uniqueness

B. S3 has a request rate limit per prefix and the high volume of PUT requests is being throttled, causing some writes to fail silently. Distribute writes across multiple prefixes using a randomized naming convention

C. S3 eventually consistent reads are returning stale data that makes it appear files are missing when they actually exist. Wait longer before querying for newly written files to allow consistency to propagate

D. The S3 bucket has reached its maximum object count limit and new objects are being rejected. Create additional buckets and distribute log writes across them to stay under the per-bucket object limit
explanation:
Multiple instances are is correct because if multiple instances write objects with the same key name simultaneously, S3 will store only the last write, causing.
S3 has a is incorrect because S3 supports thousands of PUT requests per second per prefix and automatically scales, making throttling.
S3 eventually consistent is incorrect because including the instance ID and timestamp in the key ensures unique object names.
The S3 bucket is incorrect because S3 has no practical limit on the number of objects in a bucket.

question: A company hosts a static website on S3 with CloudFront as the CDN. The website serves users globally. During an S3 Regional outage, the entire website becomes unavailable because CloudFront cannot reach the origin. The company needs the website to remain available during S3 outages. Which architecture improves availability?
options: A. Configure CloudFront to increase its default TTL to 7 days so that all website content remains cached at edge locations for an extended period, eliminating the need to contact the S3 origin during outages
B. Switch from S3 static website hosting to hosting the website on an EC2 instance behind an ALB, which provides more resilient origin hosting than S3 for CloudFront distributions

explanation:

question: A financial company needs to upload 50 TB of historical data to S3 within one week. The company has a 1 Gbps Direct Connect link that is already 70 percent utilized by production traffic. question: An order processing system uses SQS standard queues to decouple the web tier from the processing tier. During peak sales events, the processing tier falls behind and orders take hours to process. The company needs to automatically scale processing capacity based on queue depth. Which solution achieves this?
options: A. Configure SQS long polling with a 20-second wait time and increase the number of messages each instance retrieves per poll from 1 to 10 so that each existing instance processes messages faster during peak periods

B. Switch from SQS standard queues to FIFO queues which process messages more efficiently due to their ordered delivery guarantee, reducing the time required to process each individual order in the queue

explanation:

question: A company uses SQS to queue payment processing requests. Some payments fail due to temporary bank API outages. Failed payments are currently lost because the processing application deletes the message before confirming payment success. Which approach ensures failed payments are retried without losing any messages?
options: A. Increase the message retention period on the SQS queue from 4 days to 14 days so that messages remain in the queue longer, giving the bank API more time to recover before messages expire and are deleted

B. Create a duplicate SQS queue and publish every payment message to both queues simultaneously, processing from the primary queue and using the secondary queue as a backup if messages fail in the primary
C. Store a copy of every payment message in a DynamoDB table before publishing to SQS, then run a scheduled job that checks for unprocessed payments in DynamoDB and republishes them to the SQS queue

D. Configure the application to delete the message only after the payment is confirmed successful, and configure a dead-letter queue with a maximum receive count so persistently failing messages are moved aside
explanation:
Configure the application is correct because deleting messages only after successful processing ensures failed payments become visible again after the visibility timeout for retry.
Increase the message is incorrect because the dead-letter queue captures messages that fail repeatedly, preventing infinite retry loops while preserving them for investigation.
Create a duplicate is incorrect because duplicate queues add complexity and do not address the root cause of premature message deletion.

question: A document management system allows users to upload files which are then processed asynchronously. The processing involves virus scanning, format conversion, and metadata extraction. Each step must complete before the next begins, and if any step fails, the entire pipeline should not proceed. Which architecture coordinates this sequential processing?
options: A. Use a single SQS queue and implement all three processing steps in a single Lambda function that executes them sequentially, deleting the message only if all three steps complete without any errors occurring

B. Create a fan-out pattern with an SNS topic that distributes each upload to three SQS queues simultaneously, with each queue feeding a separate processing step that runs independently and in parallel with others

C. Create three separate SQS queues chained together where each processing step reads from one queue, performs its work, and if successful publishes a message to the next queue in the sequence to trigger the next step

D. Use Amazon EventBridge to trigger all three processing steps as separate targets of a single rule, relying on EventBridge to manage the ordering and retry logic for each step automatically in sequence
explanation:
Create three separate is correct because chaining SQS queues ensures each step only proceeds when the previous step succeeds and publishes to the next queue.
Use a single is incorrect because a single Lambda function running all three steps creates a monolithic processor that may exceed.
Create a fan-out is incorrect because if any step fails, the message remains in its current queue for retry without triggering downstream steps.
Use Amazon EventBridge is incorrect because EventBridge does not natively manage sequential ordering of multiple targets from a single rule and.

question: A company processes customer support tickets using an SQS FIFO queue to ensure tickets from the same customer are handled in the order they were submitted. Processing takes 2 to 5 minutes per ticket. The team notices that when one customer submits many tickets, processing of tickets from other customers is blocked. Which change resolves this issue?
options: A. Use the message group ID to assign each customer a unique group so that tickets within a customer group are ordered while tickets from different customers can be processed concurrently across message groups

explanation:

Switch from a is incorrect because switching to a standard queue loses the ordering guarantee entirely and application-level ordering adds significant.
Increase the number is incorrect because assigning each customer a unique group ID means one customer's tickets do not block others.

question: A company uses SQS to decouple a web application from a backend processing service. The messages contain sensitive customer data including credit card numbers. Compliance requires that message content be encrypted at rest. Which configuration meets this requirement with the least operational overhead?
options: A. Implement client-side encryption in the producer application using the AWS Encryption SDK to encrypt messages before sending them to SQS, and decrypt in the consumer application after receiving the messages

B. Create a VPC endpoint for SQS and configure the endpoint policy to require TLS 1.2 for all connections, which encrypts messages during transit and satisfies the at-rest encryption compliance requirement

C. Store the sensitive data in an encrypted DynamoDB table and send only a reference key in the SQS message, so the consumer retrieves the sensitive data from DynamoDB using the key from the message body

D. Enable SQS server-side encryption using an AWS managed KMS key which automatically encrypts all messages at rest and decrypts them when consumed, requiring no changes to the producer or consumer applications
explanation:
Enable SQS server-side is correct because SQS server-side encryption with an AWS managed KMS key encrypts all messages at rest transparently without requiring any code.
Implement client-side encryption is incorrect because client-side encryption requires code changes in both the producer and consumer applications and the operational.
Create a VPC is incorrect because TLS encrypts data in transit, not at rest, so it does not meet the compliance.
