question: A notification system sends messages to an SQS queue for delivery to multiple downstream services. Each service needs to receive every message independently. Currently, once one service reads and deletes a message, other services cannot access it. Which architecture allows all services to receive every message?
options: A. Create separate SQS queues for each downstream service and have the producer application send a copy of every message to each queue individually, ensuring every service gets its own copy of each message

B. Change the SQS queue visibility timeout to zero seconds so that messages remain visible to all consumers simultaneously and each service can read the message without it becoming invisible to others

D. Use an SNS topic as the entry point and subscribe each downstream service SQS queue to the topic, so that every message published to the topic is delivered to all subscribed queues independently
explanation:
Use an SNS is correct because the SNS fan-out pattern publishes each message to all subscribed SQS queues independently, ensuring every downstream service receives its.
Create separate SQS is incorrect because having the producer send messages to multiple queues individually creates tight coupling between the producer.
Change the SQS is incorrect because a zero visibility timeout would cause all consumers to receive the same message but processing.
Configure SQS message is incorrect because SQS messages are designed for single-consumer processing and coordinating deletion across multiple services using attributes.

question: A company runs a data ingestion pipeline where producers send 50 KB JSON payloads to an SQS queue. The company plans to increase the payload size to 1 MB. question: A company uses SQS to buffer requests between a frontend API and a backend service. During a backend outage lasting two hours, messages accumulated in the queue. question: A social media application uses DynamoDB to store user profiles. question: A global e-commerce company needs to provide sub-10-millisecond read and write latency for its product catalog stored in DynamoDB. The application serves customers in North America, Europe, and Asia Pacific. Which DynamoDB feature provides low-latency access from all three regions?

question: A content management system runs on multiple EC2 instances behind an ALB. Each instance needs access to the same set of uploaded media files. question: A company uses Amazon EFS for shared storage across its application fleet. The file system stores a mix of frequently accessed configuration files and rarely accessed log archives. question: A machine learning team processes large datasets stored on Amazon EFS. Training jobs run on a cluster of EC2 instances that read the same dataset files concurrently. question: A company runs a serverless application that uses AWS Lambda functions to process files. question: A company operates a web application that stores user uploads on Amazon EFS. question: A company runs its microservices application on Amazon ECS with Fargate launch type. During traffic spikes, some tasks take 3 minutes to start because container images are large. This startup delay causes request timeouts for users. Which approach reduces task startup time?

question: A company deploys a web application on ECS Fargate with tasks running in a single AZ. During a recent AZ outage, all tasks became unavailable. The architect must redesign the deployment to survive AZ failures. Which configuration provides AZ-level resilience for Fargate tasks?

question: A company runs a batch processing application on ECS with Fargate. The application needs to process files from S3 and write results back to S3. question: A company needs to trigger different actions when an EC2 instance changes state. When an instance enters the running state, a configuration management tool should be invoked. Increase the SNS is incorrect because increasing throughput limits does not provide delivery prioritization and both message types still compete for.

Enable SNS message is incorrect because SNS does not support message batching for delivery, and combining notifications would not prioritize alert.

question: A company operates a latency-sensitive trading application deployed in us-east-1 and eu-west-1. Users across the globe connect directly to Regional ALB endpoints, and those far from both Regions experience high latency. The company needs to reduce network latency for all users without changing the application architecture. Which service addresses this?
options: A. Configure AWS Global Accelerator with endpoints in both Regions so that user traffic enters the AWS global network at the nearest edge location and is routed over the optimized AWS backbone to the closest healthy Region

B. Deploy Amazon CloudFront distributions in front of the ALBs in both Regions and configure the distributions to cache API responses at edge locations, reducing round-trip latency for repeated requests globally

C. Create Route 53 latency-based routing records pointing to the ALBs in both Regions so that DNS resolution directs users to the Region with the lowest network latency based on the resolver geographic location

D. Deploy additional ALBs in four more Regions and use Route 53 geolocation routing to direct users to the nearest Regional ALB, reducing the distance between users and the application entry point globally
explanation:
Configure AWS Global is correct because Global Accelerator uses AWS edge locations to onramp user traffic to the AWS global network at the nearest point.
Deploy Amazon CloudFront is incorrect because CloudFront is designed for cacheable content and a trading application with dynamic, personalized API responses.
Create Route 53 is incorrect because latency-based routing improves DNS resolution but traffic still travels over the public internet, which is.
Deploy additional ALBs is incorrect because deploying the application in six Regions significantly increases operational complexity and cost while still routing.

question: A gaming company uses Global Accelerator to route players to the nearest Regional game server. The company is launching a new Region in ap-southeast-1 and wants to gradually shift traffic to the new Region to validate performance before routing all Asia Pacific players there. Which Global Accelerator feature supports this gradual migration?
options: A. Configure endpoint group traffic dials to control the percentage of traffic routed to the new ap-southeast-1 endpoint group, starting at a low percentage and increasing it as confidence in the new Region grows

B. Create a separate Global Accelerator accelerator for the new Region and use Route 53 weighted routing between the two accelerator DNS names to gradually shift traffic from the existing accelerator to the new one

C. Add the new Region endpoints to the existing endpoint group and configure endpoint weights to send a small percentage of traffic to the new endpoints while the majority continues to the established Region endpoints

D. Use Global Accelerator custom routing to define specific port mappings that direct a subset of player connections to the new Region based on their source port range while routing other players to existing Regions
explanation:
Configure endpoint group is correct because the traffic dial on an endpoint group allows controlling the percentage of traffic that Global Accelerator routes to that.
Create a separate is incorrect because creating a separate accelerator and using Route 53 routing adds complexity and loses the benefits.
Add the new is incorrect because endpoint weights control traffic distribution within an endpoint group, not between groups, and all endpoints.
Use Global Accelerator is incorrect because custom routing accelerators are designed for specific use cases like game servers where clients need.

question: A financial services company requires static IP addresses for its global API endpoints so that partner organizations can add them to firewall allow lists. The API is deployed behind ALBs in three Regions. Partners currently maintain separate allow lists for each Regional ALB IP, which changes periodically. Which solution provides stable static IPs for all Regions?
options: A. Deploy AWS Global Accelerator which provides two static anycast IP addresses that serve as the global entry point, routing traffic to the nearest healthy Regional ALB without partners needing Regional IPs
B. Assign Elastic IP addresses to each Regional ALB and provide these EIPs to partners for their firewall allow lists, since EIPs remain static unless explicitly released by the account owner

C. Configure Route 53 with an alias record pointing to each ALB and provide the Route 53 DNS name to partners, since DNS names remain constant even when the underlying ALB IP addresses change periodically

D. Deploy Network Load Balancers with Elastic IPs in each Region and place them in front of the ALBs, providing partners with the three sets of NLB static IPs for their firewall allow lists across all Regions
explanation:
Deploy AWS Global is correct because Global Accelerator provides two static anycast IP addresses that never change and serve as a single global entry point.
Assign Elastic IP is incorrect because ALBs do not support Elastic IP assignment, as ALB IP addresses are managed dynamically by.
Configure Route 53 is incorrect because partners need IP addresses for firewall allow lists and DNS names cannot be used in.
Deploy Network Load is incorrect because placing NLBs in front of ALBs in three Regions still requires partners to maintain allow.

question: A company uses Global Accelerator for its multi-Region application. The us-east-1 endpoint group experiences a partial failure where the ALB is healthy but serving errors for some request paths. Global Accelerator health checks only verify basic connectivity. The company needs more granular failover control. Which approach provides application-aware failover?
options: A. Configure Global Accelerator health checks to use a custom health check endpoint that validates critical application paths and returns unhealthy when any monitored path fails, triggering failover to another Region

B. Deploy a Lambda function that periodically tests critical application paths and uses the Global Accelerator API to remove the unhealthy endpoint group when failures are detected on specific request paths

C. Use Route 53 health checks in front of Global Accelerator that monitor specific application paths, and configure Route 53 to update Global Accelerator endpoint weights automatically based on health check results

D. Configure the ALB health check to verify all critical application paths and rely on Global Accelerator to detect the ALB target group health status, which triggers automatic failover to the secondary Region endpoint
explanation:
Deploy a Lambda is correct because a Lambda function can perform deep application health checks on specific paths and use the Global Accelerator API to.
Configure Global Accelerator is incorrect because Route 53 health checks cannot directly modify Global Accelerator endpoint configurations, and the two services.
Use Route 53 is incorrect because Global Accelerator health checks support basic TCP or HTTP checks but cannot monitor multiple specific.
Configure the ALB is incorrect because ALB target group health checks determine individual target health within the ALB, but Global Accelerator.

question: A multiplayer game company uses Global Accelerator to route players to game server EC2 instances. Each game session runs on a specific instance and players must connect to that exact instance, not be load balanced. The company needs Global Accelerator to provide static IPs while allowing deterministic routing to specific instances. Which accelerator type supports this?
options: A. Use a standard Global Accelerator with endpoint weights set to route 100 percent of traffic to a single instance within each endpoint group, reconfiguring the weights when new game sessions are created

B. Use Global Accelerator custom routing which maps specific port ranges to specific EC2 instances, allowing the game matchmaking service to assign players a port that deterministically routes them to their game server

C. Use a standard Global Accelerator with multiple listener ports, one per game server instance, and configure each listener to forward traffic to a single-instance endpoint group for deterministic routing

D. Use a standard Global Accelerator with client affinity set to SOURCE_IP so that once a player connects to a specific game server instance, all subsequent traffic from that player routes to the same instance
explanation:
Use Global Accelerator is correct because Global Accelerator custom routing accelerators provide deterministic mapping from specific port ranges on the static accelerator IPs to specific.
Use a standard Global Accelerator with endpoint is incorrect because standard accelerators have limits on the number of listeners and this approach does not scale.
Use a standard Global Accelerator with multiple is incorrect because SOURCE_IP affinity routes based on client IP but the initial routing is still load-balanced, meaning.
Use a standard Global Accelerator with client is incorrect because reconfiguring endpoint weights for each game session is operationally complex and does not provide the.

question: A company runs critical on-premises applications and needs a disaster recovery solution that provides an RPO of seconds and an RTO of minutes. The company wants to minimize the cost of maintaining DR infrastructure in AWS when no disaster is occurring. Which service meets these requirements?
options: A. Deploy identical production infrastructure in AWS using CloudFormation and keep it running at all times in a warm standby configuration, using Route 53 failover routing to switch traffic during a disaster event
B. Create nightly AMI snapshots of all on-premises servers using the VM Import/Export service and store them in AWS, launching instances from the most recent AMIs during a disaster for recovery

C. Use AWS Backup to create hourly backups of on-premises servers and store them in S3, restoring from the latest backup to new EC2 instances when a disaster occurs to recover the application environment

D. Use AWS Elastic Disaster Recovery to continuously replicate on-premises servers to AWS with lightweight staging instances, launching full-sized recovery instances only when a disaster is declared to minimize idle costs
explanation:
Use AWS Elastic is correct because AWS Elastic Disaster Recovery continuously replicates data at the block level providing seconds of RPO, uses low-cost staging instances.
Deploy identical production is incorrect because keeping full production infrastructure running at all times in warm standby is significantly more expensive.
Create nightly AMI is incorrect because nightly AMI creation provides an RPO of up to 24 hours, far exceeding the seconds.
Use AWS Backup is incorrect because hourly backups provide an RPO of up to one hour and restoration from backups takes.

question: A company uses AWS Elastic Disaster Recovery to protect its on-premises VMware environment. The team wants to verify that their DR plan works correctly before an actual disaster. Testing must not affect the production environment or the ongoing replication. Which Elastic Disaster Recovery capability supports this requirement?
options: A. Pause replication temporarily and launch recovery instances from the current replication state, conduct testing on the launched instances, then resume replication after the test is complete and results are validated

B. Initiate a DR drill that launches recovery instances from the replicated data in an isolated VPC, allowing the team to test the recovered environment without interrupting ongoing replication or affecting production

C. Create manual EBS snapshots of the replication volumes and launch EC2 instances from these snapshots in a separate account, testing the recovery procedure while replication continues to the original volumes

D. Redirect a small percentage of production traffic to the AWS DR environment using Route 53 weighted routing to validate that the replicated servers can handle real production traffic without fully failing over
explanation:
Initiate a DR is correct because Elastic Disaster Recovery supports non-disruptive DR drills that launch test instances from replicated data without affecting ongoing replication or.
Pause replication temporarily is incorrect because pausing replication creates a gap in data protection during the test period, which is risky.
Create manual EBS is incorrect because manually creating snapshots and launching instances in another account adds significant operational complexity compared to.
Redirect a small is incorrect because sending production traffic to the DR environment during a test could expose users to an.

question: A company has completed a disaster recovery failover to AWS using Elastic Disaster Recovery. Production is now running on AWS recovery instances. The on-premises data center has been repaired and the company wants to fail back to the original on-premises environment. Which approach returns operations to on-premises?
options: A. Use Elastic Disaster Recovery failback feature to reverse replication from the AWS recovery instances back to the on-premises servers, then perform a cutover back to on-premises once replication is synchronized

B. Create AMIs of the AWS recovery instances and use VM Import/Export to convert them to VMware virtual machine images, then deploy the converted images on the repaired on-premises VMware infrastructure

C. Set up a new Elastic Disaster Recovery configuration that treats the on-premises environment as the target and AWS as the source, initiating fresh replication from AWS to on-premises before cutting over
D. Manually copy all data from the AWS recovery instances to the on-premises servers using AWS DataSync over a Direct Connect link, then shut down the AWS instances and resume operations on-premises
explanation:
Use Elastic Disaster is correct because Elastic Disaster Recovery includes a native failback capability that reverses the replication direction from AWS back to the on-premises.
Create AMIs of is incorrect because VM Import/Export conversion is a manual process that does not capture real-time changes and may.
Set up a is incorrect because setting up a new configuration from scratch is unnecessary when the failback feature is built.
Manually copy all is incorrect because manual data copy using DataSync does not provide the block-level synchronization needed for consistent failback.

question: A company protects 50 on-premises servers with AWS Elastic Disaster Recovery. The security team requires that all replicated data stored in AWS be encrypted using customer-managed KMS keys and that replication traffic be encrypted in transit. Which configuration meets both encryption requirements?
options: A. Configure the Elastic Disaster Recovery replication settings to use customer-managed KMS keys for EBS volume encryption on staging and recovery instances, and the service encrypts replication traffic in transit by default

B. Create encrypted EBS volumes manually using customer-managed KMS keys and configure Elastic Disaster Recovery to use these pre-created volumes as replication targets for each of the 50 on-premises servers

C. Configure the replication agent on each on-premises server to encrypt data using the customer-managed KMS key before transmitting it to AWS, and store the encrypted data on unencrypted EBS volumes in AWS

D. Use AWS CloudHSM to generate and manage encryption keys for both in-transit and at-rest encryption, configuring the Elastic Disaster Recovery replication agent to use the CloudHSM endpoint for key operations
explanation:
Configure the Elastic is correct because Elastic Disaster Recovery supports specifying customer-managed KMS keys for EBS volume encryption in the replication settings, and the service.
Create encrypted EBS is incorrect because Elastic Disaster Recovery manages replication volumes automatically and does not support using pre-created EBS volumes.
Configure the replication is incorrect because the replication agent does not support application-level encryption with KMS keys before transmission, and the.
Use AWS CloudHSM is incorrect because Elastic Disaster Recovery does not integrate with CloudHSM for replication encryption and the service provides.

question: A company uses Elastic Disaster Recovery and needs to ensure that when a failover is initiated, the recovery instances launch with the correct network configuration including specific VPC, subnets, security groups, and instance types. Where are these recovery environment settings configured?
options: A. Create a CloudFormation template that defines the VPC, subnets, security groups, and instance types, and trigger the template deployment as a post-recovery step after Elastic Disaster Recovery launches instances

B. Configure the network settings in the Elastic Disaster Recovery replication agent installed on each on-premises server, which transmits the desired AWS network configuration along with the replication data

C. Configure launch settings in the Elastic Disaster Recovery console for each source server, specifying the target VPC, subnet, security groups, and instance type that recovery instances should use when launched

D. Create an AWS Systems Manager runbook that modifies the network configuration of recovery instances after they launch, moving them to the correct VPC and attaching the specified security groups and subnets
explanation:
Configure launch settings is correct because Elastic Disaster Recovery launch settings allow configuring the target EC2 instance type, VPC, subnet, security groups, and other parameters.
Create a CloudFormation is incorrect because using CloudFormation as a post-recovery step delays proper network configuration and may leave instances temporarily.
Configure the network is incorrect because the replication agent handles data replication and does not transmit or configure AWS networking parameters.
Create an AWS is incorrect because modifying network configuration after launch introduces a window where instances are misconfigured, and you cannot.

question: A media company stores millions of image assets in Amazon S3. The application performs thousands of GET requests per second spread across random object key prefixes. Users report intermittent HTTP 503 Slow Down errors during peak hours. Which solution best improves read performance while minimizing application changes?
options: A. Enable S3 Transfer Acceleration on the bucket and update the application endpoint to use the accelerated domain for all GET requests
B. Add randomized four-character hex prefixes to object keys and re-upload all objects so that requests are spread across more S3 partitions
C. Migrate the objects to S3 Intelligent-Tiering and enable the Archive Access tier to redistribute the storage load across partitions
D. Place Amazon CloudFront in front of the S3 bucket with a high TTL cache policy so repeated object reads are served from edge locations
explanation:
Place Amazon CloudFront is correct because cloudFront caches frequently accessed objects at edge locations, offloading the majority of GET requests from S3 and eliminating throttling errors.
Enable S3 Transfer is incorrect because with Transfer Acceleration speeds up uploads but does not cache reads or reduce request rates on S3.
Add randomized four-character is incorrect because with randomized prefixes was a legacy best practice but S3 now automatically partitions by prefix, and re-uploading millions.
Migrate the objects is incorrect because with Intelligent-Tiering manages storage cost tiers and does not affect request throughput.

question: A genomics research firm uploads multi-terabyte sequencing datasets from on-premises servers to Amazon S3 over a 1 Gbps Direct Connect link. Single-object uploads frequently time out before completing. Which approach maximizes upload throughput and reliability for these large files?
options: A. Enable S3 Versioning on the destination bucket and retry each failed upload so that partial data is preserved and can be resumed later
B. Use S3 multipart upload with a part size of 100 MB and configure parallel thread uploads to maximize bandwidth utilization across the link
C. Compress each dataset into a single gzip archive before uploading to reduce the amount of data transferred across the Direct Connect link
D. Switch from Direct Connect to an AWS Site-to-Site VPN connection which provides built-in packet retransmission for large object transfers
explanation:
Use S3 multipart is correct because S3 multipart upload breaks large objects into smaller parts that upload in parallel, maximizing throughput and allowing failed parts to retry independently.
Enable S3 Versioning is incorrect because with versioning does not resume partial uploads since a failed PutObject must restart from the beginning.
Compress each dataset is incorrect because with compression reduces size but does not solve the timeout issue for still-large compressed files and adds CPU.
Switch from Direct is incorrect because with VPN provides lower throughput than Direct Connect and does not solve the core large-file upload problem.

question: An e-commerce platform serves product images from Amazon S3 to customers worldwide. Latency for customers in Asia-Pacific is three times higher than for North American users. The company wants to reduce image load times globally without replicating the bucket to other Regions. Which solution provides the lowest latency improvement?
options: A. Enable S3 Transfer Acceleration on the bucket and update the application to generate pre-signed URLs that reference the accelerated endpoint
B. Create an Amazon CloudFront distribution with the S3 bucket as origin and configure a cache policy with a 24-hour default TTL for images
C. Enable S3 Cross-Region Replication to an Asia-Pacific Region bucket and use Route 53 latency-based routing to direct users accordingly
D. Move the S3 bucket to a central Region such as eu-west-1 to balance the average network distance between North American and Asian users
explanation:
Create an Amazon is correct because cloudFront caches images at edge locations worldwide, dramatically reducing latency for users in all Regions without bucket replication.
Enable S3 Transfer is incorrect because with Transfer Acceleration improves upload performance but does not cache content at edges for download acceleration.
Enable S3 Cross-Region is incorrect because with Cross-Region Replication explicitly violates the constraint of not replicating the bucket to other Regions.
Move the S3 is incorrect because moves the bucket farther from North American users without guaranteeing improvement for Asian users.

question: A data lake on Amazon S3 receives 50,000 new JSON log files per hour. An analytics application uses S3 Select to scan individual files, but queries are slow because each file is only 64 KB. The team wants to improve query performance without migrating away from S3. Which approach most effectively addresses the performance bottleneck?
options: A. Switch from S3 Select to using GetObject with byte-range fetches on each 64 KB JSON file to retrieve specific portions of the data faster
B. Enable S3 Intelligent-Tiering on the bucket to optimize the storage class of each small file for the most cost-effective retrieval pattern
C. Convert the small JSON files into larger columnar Apache Parquet files using a periodic aggregation job and query the consolidated Parquet files
D. Enable server-side encryption with AWS KMS on every object so that S3 caches the decrypted content in memory for faster repeated access
explanation:
Convert the small is correct because aggregating many small JSON files into larger Parquet files reduces the per-request overhead dramatically and Parquet columnar format enables efficient column pruning.
Switch from S3 is incorrect because with byte-range fetches on 64 KB files provides no benefit since the files are already tiny.
Enable S3 Intelligent-Tiering is incorrect because with Intelligent-Tiering manages storage costs and does not improve query performance on small files.
Enable server-side encryption is incorrect because with KMS encryption adds processing overhead and does not cache decrypted content.

question: A financial services application writes daily reconciliation reports as 500 MB CSV files to Amazon S3. Downstream systems must read these files within seconds of creation. Occasionally the reading application retrieves a partial file because it starts reading before the upload completes. Which design change guarantees that downstream systems only read complete files?
options: A. Enable S3 Versioning so that the reading application can always retrieve the most recent complete version of the file from the bucket
B. Write the file to a temporary key prefix and use S3 Event Notifications to trigger a Lambda function that copies the completed file to the final key
C. Enable S3 Object Lock in governance mode on the bucket to prevent any read access until the object has been fully written to the bucket
D. Increase the read timeout on the downstream application to 60 seconds so that S3 has enough time to finish storing the object before serving it
explanation:
Write the file is correct because writing to a temporary prefix and copying to the final location via an event-triggered Lambda ensures the file at the final key.
Enable S3 Versioning is incorrect because with versioning does not prevent reads of a partially uploaded current version.
Enable S3 Object is incorrect because with Object Lock controls deletion and overwrite retention, not read timing relative to write completion.
Increase the read is incorrect because with increased read timeout does not prevent reading a partially uploaded object.

question: A SaaS company stores user-uploaded documents in Amazon S3 with SSE-S3 encryption. A new compliance mandate requires that encryption keys be rotated every 90 days and all key management events be auditable in AWS CloudTrail. The team wants the least operational overhead. Which approach meets these requirements?
options: A. Create a customer-managed AWS KMS key with automatic rotation enabled and re-encrypt all objects in the bucket with the new SSE-KMS key policy
B. Continue using SSE-S3 and write a Lambda function that runs every 90 days to re-encrypt each object in place by copying it to itself with a new key
C. Switch to SSE-KMS with a customer-managed key that has automatic yearly rotation and use a bucket policy to enforce SSE-KMS on all new uploads
D. Enable S3 Bucket Key with SSE-S3 and configure a lifecycle policy to transition objects to Glacier every 90 days which re-encrypts them automatically
explanation:
Switch to SSE-KMS is correct because SSE-KMS with a customer-managed key provides CloudTrail auditing of all key usage events, and automatic rotation handles key rotation with minimal overhead.
Create a customer-managed is incorrect because requires manually re-encrypting all existing objects which is operationally expensive.
Continue using SSE-S3 is incorrect because with a Lambda-based re-encryption is complex and error-prone at scale.
Enable S3 Bucket is incorrect because with Glacier lifecycle transitions changes storage class but does not rotate encryption keys or provide key-level audit trails.

question: A video streaming service stores original source videos in Amazon S3. Each video is accessed heavily for the first 30 days after upload and then rarely accessed for the following 11 months before being permanently archived. The company wants to minimize storage costs while maintaining millisecond retrieval for recent content. Which lifecycle configuration achieves this?
options: A. Transition objects to S3 One Zone-IA after 7 days and then to S3 Glacier Flexible Retrieval at 30 days to achieve the lowest possible storage cost
B. Store all objects in S3 Intelligent-Tiering from day one and let the automatic monitoring move them between frequent and infrequent access tiers
C. Keep objects in S3 Standard for 30 days, transition to S3 Standard-IA at 30 days, and then transition to S3 Glacier Deep Archive at 365 days
D. Keep objects in S3 Standard permanently and enable S3 Object Lock retention for 365 days to protect the videos from accidental deletion or modification
explanation:
Keep objects in S3 Standard for is correct because S3 Standard for the first 30 days provides high-throughput millisecond access, Standard-IA lowers costs for infrequent access with instant.
Transition objects to is incorrect because moves to Glacier at 30 days which does not provide millisecond retrieval and One Zone-IA risks data loss.
Store all objects is incorrect because with Intelligent-Tiering works but incurs per-object monitoring fees on millions of videos and does not archive after a.
Keep objects in S3 Standard permanently is incorrect because keeps everything in Standard which is the most expensive tier.

question: A machine learning team runs daily training jobs that read a 2 TB dataset from Amazon S3. Each training job reads the entire dataset sequentially from start to finish. The team notices that the EC2 training instances in the same Region spend 40 minutes downloading the data before training can begin. Which approach best reduces the data loading time?
options: A. Copy the dataset to an Amazon EBS io2 volume before each training run and mount the volume on the training instance for local reads throughout
B. Enable requester-pays on the S3 bucket so that the training account is directly billed, which prioritizes the download bandwidth allocation
C. Store the dataset in Amazon S3 Express One Zone and configure the training instances to read from the S3 Express One Zone directory bucket
D. Convert the dataset format from CSV to compressed JSON to reduce the overall file size and therefore reduce the time needed to download it fully
explanation:
Store the dataset is correct because S3 Express One Zone provides single-digit millisecond latency and up to 10x faster throughput than S3 Standard, ideal for repeatedly reading large.
Copy the dataset is incorrect because with EBS copies adds a separate copy step and io2 provisioning overhead that may not reduce total time.
Enable requester-pays on is incorrect because with requester-pays changes billing responsibility only and does not affect download speed.
Convert the dataset is incorrect because with JSON conversion typically produces larger files than CSV and compression adds decompression overhead.

question: A healthcare application stores patient imaging files across three S3 buckets in three different AWS Regions to meet data residency requirements. A central dashboard must query metadata across all three buckets and return unified results. The team wants to simplify multi-Region access without modifying the application code that references each Regional bucket. Which feature addresses this?
options: A. Enable S3 Inventory reports on all three buckets and aggregate the inventory CSV files into Amazon Athena for the dashboard to query centrally
B. Configure S3 Cross-Region Replication from all three buckets into a fourth centralized bucket and point the dashboard at that single bucket
C. Create an S3 Multi-Region Access Point that spans all three buckets and update the dashboard to use the single Multi-Region Access Point ARN
D. Use S3 Batch Operations to copy all metadata objects from the three buckets into a single DynamoDB table that the dashboard queries for results
explanation:
Create an S3 is correct because S3 Multi-Region Access Point provides a single global endpoint that routes requests to the closest bucket, simplifying multi-Region access without changing bucket.
Enable S3 Inventory is incorrect because with Inventory and Athena adds complexity and latency and does not provide direct S3 access.
Configure S3 Cross-Region is incorrect because with replication into a fourth bucket violates data residency requirements by centralizing copies.
Use S3 Batch is incorrect because with Batch Operations and DynamoDB adds significant operational complexity and data duplication.

question: A log aggregation system writes 10,000 small objects per second to a single Amazon S3 bucket. The development team wants to capture every new object creation event and process it with a Lambda function. They discover that some events are occasionally missed under heavy load. Which architecture change ensures all events are captured reliably?
options: A. Replace S3 Event Notifications with S3 Inventory reports that run hourly and trigger Lambda to process any objects not yet marked as processed
B. Enable Amazon S3 Event Notifications to publish to an Amazon SQS queue and configure the Lambda function to poll the SQS queue for new messages
C. Increase the Lambda function concurrency limit to 10,000 so that every event notification is accepted without being throttled by the Lambda service
D. Switch from S3 Event Notifications to polling the S3 bucket every second using a scheduled Lambda function that lists all newly created objects
explanation:
Enable Amazon S3 is correct because sending S3 events to SQS provides a durable buffer that retains messages until Lambda successfully processes them, preventing event loss under high.
Replace S3 Event is incorrect because with hourly Inventory reports introduces unacceptable delay and does not capture events in real time.
Increase the Lambda is incorrect because with higher concurrency does not fix dropped notifications since the issue is event delivery not Lambda throttling.
Switch from S3 is incorrect because with polling via ListObjects is expensive and slow at 10,000 objects per second and may still miss objects.

question: A company runs an image-processing Lambda function that takes 8 seconds per invocation. The function loads a 200 MB machine learning model from S3 on every cold start, adding 15 seconds of latency. Invocations are steady at 50 per minute. Which approach most effectively reduces cold-start latency for this function?
options: A. Package the ML model inside a Lambda layer and increase the function memory to 2048 MB so the model loads from the local deployment package faster
B. Enable Provisioned Concurrency with a minimum of 10 instances to keep warm execution environments ready with the model already loaded in memory
C. Reduce the Lambda function timeout from 60 seconds to 20 seconds so the service recycles execution environments more frequently and keeps them warm
D. Move the ML model to an Amazon EFS file system mounted on the Lambda function to increase the download speed compared to pulling from S3 each time
explanation:
Enable Provisioned Concurrency is correct because provisioned Concurrency pre-initializes execution environments so the model is loaded before any request arrives, eliminating cold-start latency entirely.
Package the ML is incorrect because with a Lambda layer still requires the model to load during cold starts and layers have a 250.
Reduce the Lambda is incorrect because with a shorter timeout does not prevent cold starts and may cause function failures.
Move the ML is incorrect because with EFS reduces download time but still incurs loading latency on each cold start.

question: A real-time data enrichment pipeline processes messages from Amazon SQS. Each Lambda invocation queries a PostgreSQL database on Amazon RDS, but the database reaches its maximum connection limit during traffic spikes causing function errors. Which solution resolves the connection exhaustion without increasing the RDS instance size?
options: A. Switch the RDS database from PostgreSQL to Amazon Aurora Serverless v1 which automatically scales connections without any connection limit ceiling
B. Increase the Lambda function reserved concurrency to 1000 to ensure each invocation gets a dedicated database connection without contention
C. Place Amazon RDS Proxy between the Lambda function and the RDS database to pool and share database connections across concurrent invocations
D. Add a five-second sleep at the beginning of each Lambda invocation to stagger connection attempts and reduce the peak number of simultaneous connections
explanation:
Place Amazon RDS is correct because RDS Proxy manages a connection pool that multiplexes many Lambda invocations over fewer database connections, preventing connection exhaustion.
Switch the RDS is incorrect because with Aurora Serverless v1 still has connection limits and a migration introduces significant effort.
Increase the Lambda is incorrect because with sleep delays increases function duration and cost without guaranteeing connection reduction.
Add a five-second is incorrect because with higher concurrency creates even more concurrent connections, worsening the problem.

question: A serverless application uses AWS Lambda with 128 MB of memory. The function performs CPU-intensive JSON parsing and serialization, and average execution time is 12 seconds. The team needs to reduce execution time to under 4 seconds. Which change is most likely to achieve this goal?
options: A. Rewrite the function from Python to Go to leverage compiled language performance and reduce JSON parsing overhead significantly at the same memory
B. Increase the Lambda function memory allocation to 1024 MB which proportionally increases the CPU power allocated to the execution environment
C. Enable AWS X-Ray tracing on the Lambda function to identify the slowest code paths and then optimize only those specific bottleneck areas in the code
D. Add a Lambda layer containing a pre-compiled JSON parsing library to avoid the overhead of loading the parsing module from the deployment package itself
explanation:
Increase the Lambda is correct because lambda allocates CPU proportionally to memory, so increasing from 128 MB to 1024 MB provides 8x more CPU which directly reduces CPU-bound.
Rewrite the function is incorrect because with a language rewrite may help but is a major effort and does not guarantee a 3x improvement.
Enable AWS X-Ray is incorrect because with X-Ray identifies bottlenecks but does not itself reduce execution time.
Add a Lambda is incorrect because with a pre-compiled layer marginally reduces cold-start time but not CPU-bound processing time.

question: An event-driven application triggers a Lambda function via Amazon SNS whenever a new order is placed. The function must call three independent downstream microservices and aggregate their responses before returning. Average latency is 9 seconds because each call runs sequentially. How should the developer reduce the total execution time?
options: A. Split the Lambda function into three separate functions each triggered by the same SNS topic and use a Step Functions workflow to aggregate results
B. Use asynchronous concurrent calls within the single Lambda function to invoke all three microservices in parallel and then await all three responses
C. Create an SQS queue for each microservice and have the Lambda function send messages to all three queues instead of making synchronous HTTP calls
D. Increase the Lambda timeout to 30 seconds and add retry logic with exponential backoff on each sequential microservice call to improve overall reliability
explanation:
Use asynchronous concurrent is correct because making all three independent HTTP calls concurrently within the same function reduces total latency from the sum to the maximum of the.
Split the Lambda is incorrect because with Step Functions adds orchestration complexity and still requires aggregation logic with higher latency.
Create an SQS is incorrect because with SQS queues converts to asynchronous processing and cannot aggregate responses synchronously for the caller.
Increase the Lambda is incorrect because with a longer timeout and retries does not reduce the baseline sequential latency.

question: A logistics company processes GPS tracking events from a fleet of 5,000 vehicles. Events arrive on an Amazon Kinesis Data Stream with 10 shards. A Lambda function consumes the stream but falls behind during peak hours when event volume triples. Which configuration change helps the function keep up with the increased throughput?
options: A. Enable the Lambda event source mapping tumbling window feature with a 30-second window to batch more records per invocation and reduce overhead
B. Increase the parallelization factor on the Lambda event source mapping to process multiple batches per shard concurrently during peak periods
C. Reduce the Lambda batch size to 1 record per invocation so that each event is processed immediately without waiting for a full batch to accumulate
D. Decrease the Kinesis data retention period from 24 hours to 1 hour so that the stream discards older records and reduces the processing backlog
explanation:
Increase the parallelization is correct because increasing the parallelization factor allows multiple Lambda invocations to process batches from the same shard concurrently, increasing throughput without adding shards.
Enable the Lambda is incorrect because with tumbling windows is for aggregation use cases and does not increase processing throughput.
Reduce the Lambda is incorrect because with batch size of 1 increases invocation overhead and reduces efficiency.
Decrease the Kinesis is incorrect because with reduced retention causes data loss for unprocessed records instead of processing them.

question: A company deploys a Lambda function packaged as a 1.5 GB container image. The function runs infrequently, about twice per day, and each cold start takes 25 seconds due to the large image size. The team wants to keep the container packaging model but reduce cold-start time. What should they do?
options: A. Schedule a CloudWatch Events rule to invoke the function with a dummy payload every 5 minutes to keep the execution environment perpetually warm
B. Convert the function from a container image to a standard ZIP deployment package stored in S3 to eliminate container image pull latency entirely
C. Reduce the container image size by using a minimal base image and multi-stage builds to remove unnecessary dependencies and build artifacts
D. Increase the function ephemeral storage from 512 MB to 10 GB so that the container image layers can be cached locally between cold start events
explanation:
Reduce the container is correct because reducing the container image size directly decreases the amount of data Lambda must pull and decompress on cold start, significantly lowering initialization.
Schedule a CloudWatch is incorrect because with keep-warm invocations every 5 minutes is wasteful for a function that runs only twice daily and does.
Convert the function is incorrect because with increased ephemeral storage does not affect container image caching which is managed by the Lambda service.
Increase the function is incorrect because abandons the container packaging model which violates the stated requirement.

question: A serverless API uses API Gateway and Lambda to process customer requests. During a flash sale, the Lambda function hits the account-level concurrency limit and returns throttling errors. The team cannot increase the account concurrency limit immediately. Which approach best mitigates the throttling?
options: A. Configure API Gateway to use a usage plan with aggressive throttling limits to reject excess requests before they reach the Lambda function at all
B. Switch from synchronous API Gateway integration to asynchronous invocation with a callback URL so Lambda processes requests from its internal queue
C. Enable API Gateway caching on GET endpoints and configure Lambda reserved concurrency for the flash-sale function to guarantee it receives capacity
D. Deploy a duplicate Lambda function in a second AWS Region and use Route 53 weighted routing to distribute API Gateway traffic across both Regions
explanation:
Enable API Gateway is correct because API Gateway caching reduces the number of Lambda invocations for repeated GET requests, and reserved concurrency guarantees the function gets a dedicated.
Configure API Gateway is incorrect because with aggressive throttling simply rejects customer requests without solving capacity.
Switch from synchronous is incorrect because with asynchronous invocation changes the API pattern and still consumes the same account concurrency.
Deploy a duplicate is incorrect because with multi-Region deployment requires significant infrastructure setup and each Region has its own concurrency limit to manage.

question: A document processing pipeline triggers a Lambda function when files are uploaded to S3. Each invocation must process files up to 5 GB, which exceeds the Lambda /tmp storage default of 512 MB. The function needs local disk space for intermediate processing. Which solution allows the function to handle these large files?
options: A. Mount an Amazon EFS file system to the Lambda function and use the EFS mount point as the working directory for reading and writing intermediate files
B. Configure the Lambda function ephemeral storage to 10 GB which provides enough local /tmp space for the intermediate processing of each 5 GB file
C. Stream the S3 object directly through the function memory without writing to disk by processing the file in 64 MB chunks sent to the output destination
D. Replace Lambda with an ECS Fargate task that has 20 GB of ephemeral storage and is triggered by the same S3 event notification for each uploaded file
explanation:
Configure the Lambda is correct because lambda supports configurable ephemeral storage up to 10 GB on the /tmp directory, which provides sufficient local space for 5 GB file.
Mount an Amazon is incorrect because with EFS works but adds network latency for every read and write operation compared to local ephemeral storage.
Stream the S3 is incorrect because with streaming assumes the processing logic supports streaming which may not be possible for all document processing operations.
Replace Lambda with is incorrect because with Fargate replaces the serverless model and introduces container management overhead.

question: A financial trading application uses Lambda functions that must execute within 100 milliseconds. The functions initialize SDK clients and database connections in the handler code, causing inconsistent latency. Some invocations complete in 50 ms while others take 800 ms. How should the team optimize for consistent low latency?
options: A. Deploy the Lambda function in a VPC with an Elastic Network Interface pre-attached to eliminate the network setup time for database connectivity
B. Set the Lambda function timeout to 100 milliseconds so that any invocation exceeding the target is terminated and automatically retried by the service
C. Move SDK client and database connection initialization outside the handler function into the global scope so they persist across warm invocations
D. Use Lambda SnapStart to take a snapshot of the initialized function state so that every invocation resumes from a pre-warmed checkpoint instantly
explanation:
Move SDK client is correct because initializing SDK clients and connections in the global scope means they are created once during the cold start and reused on all.
Deploy the Lambda is incorrect because with VPC ENI pre-attachment was a legacy issue now solved by Hyperplane but does not address application-level initialization.
Set the Lambda is incorrect because with a low timeout causes failures instead of fixing the initialization overhead.
Use Lambda SnapStart is incorrect because with SnapStart is only available for Java runtimes and may not apply to this workload.

question: A mobile backend uses Lambda functions behind API Gateway. Each function writes user activity data to DynamoDB. During load testing, the team notices that 5 percent of DynamoDB writes fail due to ProvisionedThroughputExceededException. The DynamoDB table uses provisioned capacity mode. Which solution improves write reliability without over-provisioning the table?
options: A. Implement exponential backoff with jitter in the Lambda function code and increase the function timeout to allow for retries during write throttling events
B. Add a DynamoDB Accelerator cluster in front of the table and configure the Lambda function to write through DAX to buffer the writes during spikes
C. Switch the DynamoDB table to on-demand capacity mode so that write throughput automatically scales to match the actual request volume without limits
D. Create a DynamoDB global table with replicas in two additional Regions to distribute the write load across three independent table endpoints simultaneously
explanation:
Switch the DynamoDB is correct because on-demand capacity mode automatically handles traffic spikes by scaling write capacity to match actual demand, eliminating provisioned throughput exceptions.
Implement exponential backoff is incorrect because with exponential backoff adds latency and may still fail if throttling persists.
Add a DynamoDB is incorrect because with DAX primarily accelerates reads and does not buffer writes during throughput spikes.
Create a DynamoDB is incorrect because with global tables replicates data for availability but writes to any single replica still hit that table capacity.

question: A data pipeline Lambda function processes CSV files uploaded to S3. The function parses each row, validates it, and inserts it into an Aurora PostgreSQL database. Processing a 100 MB file with 500,000 rows takes 14 minutes because rows are inserted one at a time. Which optimization most reduces processing time?
options: A. Increase Lambda memory to the maximum 10240 MB to get proportionally more CPU and network bandwidth for faster individual row insertions
B. Split each uploaded CSV file into 50 smaller files using an S3 event and fan-out pattern so that 50 Lambda functions process chunks in parallel
C. Batch the parsed rows into groups of 1000 and use multi-row INSERT statements to reduce the number of database round trips from 500,000 to 500
D. Replace Aurora PostgreSQL with DynamoDB and use BatchWriteItem to insert 25 rows at a time which provides higher throughput than relational databases
explanation:
Batch the parsed is correct because batching rows into multi-row INSERT statements dramatically reduces the number of database round trips which is the primary bottleneck, cutting processing time.
Increase Lambda memory is incorrect because with more memory adds CPU but the bottleneck is network round trips not CPU.
Split each uploaded is incorrect because with DynamoDB requires a schema redesign and abandons relational query capabilities the application may depend on.
Replace Aurora PostgreSQL is incorrect because with parallel functions adds complexity and still performs single-row inserts per function.

question: A company wants to deploy a machine learning inference function on Lambda that requires a 4 GB PyTorch model. The total deployment package including dependencies exceeds the 10 GB container image limit. Which approach allows the team to run this inference workload on Lambda?
options: A. Split the deployment into two Lambda functions where the first loads half the model and the second loads the other half and they communicate via SQS
B. Compile the PyTorch model using Amazon SageMaker Neo to produce a smaller optimized model artifact that fits within the container image size limit
C. Store the model in S3 and download it to the /tmp directory during function initialization using the 10 GB configurable ephemeral storage for the model
D. Use Lambda Layers to store the PyTorch dependencies separately from the model files allowing the total combined size to exceed the container image limit
explanation:
Store the model is correct because storing the model in S3 and loading it to ephemeral storage at initialization keeps the container image under the size limit while.
Split the deployment is incorrect because with split model across two functions is architecturally impractical for inference that needs the full model loaded.
Compile the PyTorch is incorrect because with SageMaker Neo reduces model size but may not reduce it enough and changes the model framework.
Use Lambda Layers is incorrect because with layers does not apply to container-packaged functions and layers have their own size limits.

question: A marketing platform triggers Lambda functions from an SQS queue containing email rendering jobs. Each job takes 2 to 10 seconds. During campaign launches, the queue depth grows to 100,000 messages and processing takes hours to complete. The team wants to reduce queue drain time significantly. Which change provides the fastest improvement?
options: A. Increase the SQS message visibility timeout to 30 minutes to prevent messages from being reprocessed by multiple Lambda invocations simultaneously
B. Switch from a standard SQS queue to a FIFO queue to ensure that messages are processed in the exact order they were sent for campaign consistency
C. Increase the Lambda event source mapping batch size to the maximum of 10 and increase the function reserved concurrency to 1000 for faster queue drain
D. Replace the SQS trigger with an SNS topic that fans out each message to 10 separate Lambda functions to process the same message 10 times in parallel
explanation:
Increase the Lambda is correct because larger batch sizes process more messages per invocation reducing overhead, and higher reserved concurrency allows more parallel invocations to drain the queue.
Increase the SQS is incorrect because with longer visibility timeout prevents duplicate processing but does not speed up overall throughput.
Switch from a is incorrect because with FIFO queues limits throughput to 300 messages per second and ordering is unnecessary for email rendering.
Replace the SQS is incorrect because with SNS fan-out processes the same message multiple times which wastes resources and sends duplicate emails.

question: A gaming application stores player session data in a DynamoDB table with a partition key of player_id. During tournaments, the top 100 players generate 80 percent of all read traffic, causing hot partition throttling. Which design change best distributes the read load?
options: A. Add a sort key of session_timestamp to the table to spread each player data across more items and reduce the read concentration on popular partitions
B. Implement a write-sharding pattern by appending a random suffix to the player_id partition key and aggregate results across shards when reading player data
C. Place a DynamoDB Accelerator cluster in front of the table to cache the frequently read session data for popular players and offload hot partition reads
D. Enable DynamoDB auto scaling with a target utilization of 50 percent so the service provisions additional capacity units when hot partitions are detected
explanation:
Place a DynamoDB is correct because DAX caches frequently accessed items in memory, serving repeated reads for popular players from the cache and eliminating hot partition pressure on.
Add a sort is incorrect because with a sort key does not change the partition key distribution so reads still hit the same partitions.
Implement a write-sharding is incorrect because with auto scaling distributes capacity across partitions but does not eliminate per-partition throughput limits for hot keys.
Enable DynamoDB auto is incorrect because with write sharding complicates reads by requiring scatter-gather queries across all shards for each player lookup.

question: An IoT platform ingests sensor readings into a DynamoDB table using a partition key of device_id and sort key of timestamp. Queries always retrieve the latest 24 hours of readings for a given device. Over time the table grows to billions of items and storage costs increase. Which strategy maintains query performance while managing table size?
options: A. Enable DynamoDB Streams and trigger a Lambda function that deletes items older than 24 hours after they are written to the stream for archival processing
B. Set a time-to-live attribute on each item with a value 24 hours after the reading timestamp so DynamoDB automatically removes expired items at no cost
C. Create a DynamoDB global secondary index on timestamp alone and query the index instead of the base table to improve retrieval speed for recent readings
D. Partition the data into monthly DynamoDB tables and update the application to query the current month table only, deleting older tables after archiving
explanation:
Set a time-to-live is correct because TTL automatically deletes expired items in the background at no additional cost, keeping the table size manageable without application-level deletion logic.
Enable DynamoDB Streams is incorrect because with Streams-triggered deletion adds Lambda invocation costs and complexity.
Create a DynamoDB is incorrect because with a GSI on timestamp does not reduce table size and the GSI duplicates all items.
Partition the data is incorrect because with monthly tables requires application code changes for table routing and increases operational complexity.

question: A retail application uses a DynamoDB table to store product catalog data. The table has 50,000 items and the application runs full table scans to find products matching multiple filter criteria. Scan operations consume significant read capacity and take several seconds. Which approach improves query performance most effectively?
options: A. Export the table to S3 using DynamoDB export and query the exported data with Amazon Athena to leverage distributed SQL processing for complex filters
B. Enable DynamoDB Accelerator and cache the entire product catalog in memory so that all filter operations are served from the DAX cluster instead
C. Increase the provisioned read capacity units to 10,000 so that scan operations complete faster by consuming more capacity in parallel during execution
D. Create a global secondary index with a partition key matching the most common filter attribute and use Query operations on the index instead of scans
explanation:
Create a global is correct because A GSI with the most common filter attribute as partition key allows targeted Query operations that read only matching items instead of.
Export the table is incorrect because with Athena exports adds latency from the export process and is not suitable for real-time product queries.
Enable DynamoDB Accelerator is incorrect because with DAX caches individual item lookups and does not optimize scan-based filter operations.
Increase the provisioned is incorrect because with higher RCUs makes scans faster but still reads the entire table which is wasteful and expensive.

question: An e-commerce checkout system must atomically update inventory counts and create order records in DynamoDB. Currently the application uses two separate PutItem and UpdateItem calls, which occasionally creates orphaned orders when the second call fails. Which DynamoDB feature solves this consistency problem?
options: A. Write both items to the same partition key so they are stored in the same physical partition and benefit from DynamoDB internal consistency guarantees
B. Enable DynamoDB Streams and create a Lambda function that rolls back the first operation if the second operation fails within a five-second error window
C. Implement optimistic locking using a version attribute on both items and retry the entire sequence from the beginning if a conditional check expression fails
D. Use DynamoDB Transactions with a TransactWriteItems request that includes both the UpdateItem for inventory and the PutItem for the order in one atomic call
explanation:
Use DynamoDB Transactions is correct because transactWriteItems provides ACID transactions across multiple items and tables, ensuring both operations succeed or both fail atomically.
Write both items is incorrect because with same partition key does not provide transactional guarantees between separate API calls.
Enable DynamoDB Streams is incorrect because with Streams-based rollback introduces delay and the rollback itself can fail, leaving inconsistent state.
Implement optimistic locking is incorrect because with optimistic locking prevents stale writes but does not guarantee atomicity across two separate operations.

question: A social media application stores user posts in DynamoDB with user_id as the partition key. A new feature requires querying all posts across all users sorted by post_date to display a global feed. The table has 100 million items. Which approach enables the global feed query efficiently?
options: A. Create a global secondary index with a fixed value attribute as partition key and post_date as sort key to enable a sorted query across all items in the table
B. Perform a parallel Scan on the base table with a FilterExpression on post_date and sort the results in the application layer to build the global feed listing
C. Create a GSI with a date-based bucket attribute as partition key and post_date as sort key, writing each post with a bucket value derived from the post date
D. Enable DynamoDB Streams to copy all posts to an Amazon OpenSearch Service index and query OpenSearch for the globally sorted feed using date-range queries
explanation:
Create a GSI is correct because A GSI with a date-based bucket partition key distributes load across multiple partitions while allowing sorted queries by post_date within each bucket.
Create a global is incorrect because with a fixed partition key creates a hot partition since all 100 million items map to one partition.
Perform a parallel is incorrect because with parallel Scan reads the entire table and in-application sorting is extremely expensive at this scale.
Enable DynamoDB Streams is incorrect because with OpenSearch adds a separate system to manage and introduces replication lag.

question: A logistics application tracks shipment status changes in a DynamoDB table. Downstream analytics systems need to receive every status change within one second for real-time dashboard updates. The team wants a fully managed solution with minimal code. Which approach delivers near-real-time change data capture?
options: A. Configure DynamoDB point-in-time recovery and restore the table to a one-second-old snapshot every minute to compute the difference between current and past state
B. Schedule a CloudWatch Events rule to invoke a Lambda function every second that scans the DynamoDB table for items with a modified_at value in the last second
C. Create an Amazon EventBridge rule that captures DynamoDB PutItem API calls from CloudTrail and routes the event payload to the analytics system endpoint
D. Enable DynamoDB Streams on the table and configure a Lambda function trigger to read stream records and push status changes to the analytics system
explanation:
Enable DynamoDB Streams is correct because dynamoDB Streams captures item-level changes in near-real-time and a Lambda trigger processes each change record with minimal code and latency.
Configure DynamoDB point-in-time is incorrect because with point-in-time restore is designed for disaster recovery not change detection and is prohibitively expensive.
Schedule a CloudWatch is incorrect because with per-second Scans is extremely expensive and misses changes between scan intervals.
Create an Amazon is incorrect because with CloudTrail and EventBridge captures API metadata not item content, and CloudTrail delivery has multi-minute latency.

question: A multiplayer game stores leaderboard scores in DynamoDB. The application needs to retrieve the top 10 scores globally and also the rank of any individual player. The table uses player_id as the partition key. Leaderboard queries must return in under 50 milliseconds. Which design supports both access patterns efficiently?
options: A. Create a GSI with a fixed shard key and score as the sort key in descending order then query the GSI with a limit of 10 to retrieve the top scores globally
B. Maintain a separate DynamoDB table with a single item containing a sorted list of top 100 player scores that is updated by a Lambda function on each score change
C. Use a Scan operation with a FilterExpression to find scores greater than the player score and count the results to determine rank and use limit for top 10 retrieval
D. Export the leaderboard table to ElastiCache Redis using DynamoDB Streams and use Redis sorted sets for both top-N retrieval and individual rank lookups at sub-ms speed
explanation:
Export the leaderboard is correct because redis sorted sets natively support both ZRANGEBYSCORE for top-N retrieval and ZRANK for individual player ranking with sub-millisecond latency, perfectly suited for.
Create a GSI is incorrect because with a fixed shard GSI creates a hot partition problem since all items share one partition key.
Maintain a separate is incorrect because with a separate top-100 item requires complex Lambda logic and does not efficiently support arbitrary player rank lookups.
Use a Scan is incorrect because with Scan operations reads the entire table which is slow and expensive for rank calculations.

question: A content management system uses DynamoDB with on-demand capacity mode. The application experiences a sudden 10x traffic spike when a major news article goes viral. The team notices DynamoDB throttles requests for the first few minutes before adapting to the new traffic level. Which approach prevents this initial throttling?
options: A. Switch from on-demand to provisioned capacity mode with auto scaling configured to respond to consumption metrics with a scale-up cooldown of 30 seconds
B. Pre-warm the DynamoDB table by running a load test that generates the expected peak traffic level for at least five minutes before the anticipated spike occurs
C. Add an SQS queue in front of DynamoDB writes to buffer requests during the initial spike period and drain them as DynamoDB adapts to the higher throughput
D. Enable DynamoDB global tables with replicas in three Regions and distribute the viral traffic across all three table endpoints to avoid single-table throttling
explanation:
Add an SQS is correct because an SQS queue buffers the write burst so that DynamoDB receives a steady stream of requests within its current capacity while it.
Switch from on-demand is incorrect because with provisioned auto scaling still has reaction delay and does not handle instant spikes better than on-demand.
Pre-warm the DynamoDB is incorrect because with pre-warming is impractical when traffic spikes are unpredictable like viral news.
Enable DynamoDB global is incorrect because with global tables adds cross-Region replication complexity and each replica still faces the same instant-spike adaptation issue.

question: A banking application reads customer account balances from DynamoDB for display on a web dashboard. The balance must always reflect the most recent write, even if the read occurs milliseconds after an update. The team currently uses eventually consistent reads. Which change guarantees the dashboard shows the latest balance?
options: A. Configure DynamoDB Streams to push balance updates to a Lambda function that writes the latest value to an ElastiCache Redis cluster for the dashboard to read
B. Enable DynamoDB Accelerator with a write-through configuration so that every write immediately updates the DAX cache before updating the base DynamoDB table
C. Add a global secondary index on the account balance attribute and read from the index since GSI reads are always consistent with the base table writes
D. Switch to strongly consistent reads by setting ConsistentRead to true in the GetItem request to ensure the response reflects the most recent successful write
explanation:
Switch to strongly is correct because setting ConsistentRead to true guarantees that GetItem returns the most recently written value from DynamoDB.
Configure DynamoDB Streams is incorrect because with GSI reads are always eventually consistent and cannot guarantee the latest value.
Enable DynamoDB Accelerator is incorrect because with DAX write-through still serves cached reads that may be stale if the cache TTL has not expired.
Add a global is incorrect because with Streams and ElastiCache adds unnecessary complexity and Streams processing introduces its own latency.

question: An application stores 10 KB items in DynamoDB and performs 5,000 eventually consistent reads per second. The team wants to reduce read costs by half without changing the item size or read pattern. Which approach achieves this cost reduction?
options: A. Switch from eventually consistent reads to strongly consistent reads because strongly consistent reads consume fewer read capacity units per item for large items
B. Enable DynamoDB Accelerator to serve the majority of reads from the in-memory cache which does not consume any table read capacity units for cache hits
C. Create a global secondary index that projects only the attributes the application needs to read, reducing the effective item size read from the index per query
D. Compress each 10 KB item to under 4 KB before storing it in DynamoDB so that each read consumes one read capacity unit instead of three for eventual reads
explanation:
Enable DynamoDB Accelerator is correct because DAX serves cache hits from memory without consuming any DynamoDB read capacity units, which directly reduces read costs for frequently accessed data.
Switch from eventually is incorrect because strongly consistent reads consume twice the RCUs of eventually consistent reads.
Create a global is incorrect because with a GSI still consumes read capacity units from the index and adds storage cost for the projected.
Compress each 10 is incorrect because with compression reduces item size but adds application complexity and decompression latency.

question: A web application queries an RDS MySQL database for product recommendations. The same queries with identical parameters are repeated by different users throughout the day and each query takes 3 seconds. The team wants sub-millisecond response times for these repeated queries. Which caching strategy is most appropriate?
options: A. Use Amazon DynamoDB as a caching layer by writing query results to a DynamoDB table with a TTL and checking the table before querying the MySQL database
B. Enable the RDS MySQL query cache with a large buffer size so the database engine caches the results of repeated queries internally for faster responses
C. Create an Amazon CloudFront distribution with the API endpoint as origin and set a TTL of 3600 seconds to cache product recommendation HTTP responses at edge
D. Deploy an ElastiCache for Redis cluster and implement a cache-aside pattern where the application checks Redis before querying MySQL for product results
explanation:
Deploy an ElastiCache is correct because elastiCache for Redis with a cache-aside pattern provides sub-millisecond in-memory reads for repeated query results, dramatically faster than any database query.
Use Amazon DynamoDB is incorrect because with MySQL query cache is deprecated in MySQL 8.0 and provides limited performance gains compared to dedicated in-memory.
Enable the RDS is incorrect because with CloudFront caches at the HTTP layer and may serve stale data to different users who need personalized.
Create an Amazon is incorrect because with DynamoDB provides single-digit millisecond latency but not sub-millisecond like Redis.

question: An application uses ElastiCache for Redis as a session store. During peak hours, the single-node Redis instance reaches 100 percent CPU utilization and response latency increases to 50 milliseconds. Which scaling approach maintains low latency for session reads while handling the increased load?
options: A. Enable Redis Cluster Mode with multiple shards to distribute the session key space across several nodes and reduce per-node CPU utilization during peaks
B. Add Redis read replicas to the existing replication group and configure the application to direct read operations to the replicas to offload the primary node
C. Upgrade the Redis instance to a larger node type with more CPU cores and memory to handle the increased load on a single more powerful instance directly
D. Switch from ElastiCache for Redis to ElastiCache for Memcached with multiple nodes to use the multi-threaded architecture for better CPU utilization
explanation:
Add Redis read is correct because adding read replicas distributes read traffic across multiple nodes, reducing CPU load on the primary and maintaining low latency for session reads.
Enable Redis Cluster is incorrect because with Cluster Mode adds complexity for resharding existing keys and is better suited for data size scaling than.
Upgrade the Redis is incorrect because with a larger instance type has limits and may not scale proportionally for CPU-bound workloads.
Switch from ElastiCache is incorrect because with Memcached loses Redis features like persistence and data structures needed for session management.

question: A financial dashboard application caches stock price data in ElastiCache for Redis with a 60-second TTL. Users complain that the dashboard occasionally displays stale prices for up to a full minute after rapid market movements. The team wants prices to update within 5 seconds without significantly increasing cache miss rates. Which approach achieves this?
options: A. Reduce the Redis TTL from 60 seconds to 5 seconds on all cached stock price keys so stale data is evicted faster and fresh queries hit the database sooner
B. Implement an event-driven cache invalidation pattern where the price update service publishes an SNS message that triggers a Lambda to delete stale cache keys
C. Switch to a write-through caching strategy where every stock price update immediately writes the new value to both the database and the Redis cache simultaneously
D. Increase the Redis cluster node count from one to three and reduce the TTL to 30 seconds as a compromise between freshness and database load from cache misses
explanation:
Switch to a is correct because write-through caching updates Redis immediately on every price change, ensuring the cache always contains the latest value without relying on TTL expiry.
Reduce the Redis is incorrect because with more nodes and 30-second TTL still serves stale data for up to 30 seconds.
Implement an event-driven is incorrect because with 5-second TTL causes 12x more cache misses per minute, dramatically increasing database load.
Increase the Redis is incorrect because with SNS and Lambda adds latency from the event pipeline and may still exceed the 5-second requirement.

question: A microservices application uses ElastiCache for Redis to share state between services. The Redis cluster uses a single node with 32 GB of memory. Data volume is projected to grow to 200 GB within six months. The team needs a scaling strategy that accommodates growth without downtime. Which approach is recommended?
options: A. Create five separate Redis clusters each holding a different subset of service data and configure each microservice to connect to its designated cluster
B. Migrate from Redis to Memcached with 10 nodes to distribute the 200 GB dataset across the Memcached consistent hashing ring for horizontal scaling
C. Vertically scale the single Redis node to an r6g.4xlarge instance with 128 GB of memory and plan to scale vertically again when that capacity is reached
