question: A company uses Lambda to serve a machine learning inference API. The function loads a 500 MB model file from S3 during cold starts, which takes 15 seconds. Warm invocations complete in 200 milliseconds. Traffic is steady at 10 requests per second. How should they optimize?
options: A. Use Lambda Provisioned Concurrency with enough instances to handle steady traffic so the model is always loaded and cold starts never occur
B. Reduce the model file size by applying quantization and compression techniques to decrease the cold start model loading time to under 3 seconds
C. Store the model in an EFS file system mounted to the Lambda function so the model loads faster from a local file system during cold starts
D. Deploy the model on a SageMaker real-time endpoint with auto scaling which keeps the model loaded in memory and scales based on traffic volume
explanation:
Deploy the model is correct because sageMaker endpoints keep models loaded in memory, eliminating cold start loading, and auto scaling matches the steady traffic.
Use Lambda Provisioned is incorrect because works but Provisioned Concurrency for ML models is expensive.
Reduce the model is incorrect because reduces but does not eliminate the cold start problem.
Store the model is incorrect because improves loading slightly but does not eliminate the 500 MB transfer time.

question: A company serves a global website from an S3 origin. Users in Asia report slow page load times. The company currently has no CDN. Monthly S3 data transfer costs are high. Which solution addresses both issues cost-effectively?
options: A. Create an S3 bucket in an Asia Pacific region and configure Cross-Region Replication to duplicate all website content for local access by Asian users
B. Deploy a CloudFront distribution with the S3 bucket as origin to cache content at edge locations worldwide and reduce S3 data transfer charges
C. Deploy EC2 instances in an Asia Pacific region running a reverse proxy that caches and serves content from the S3 bucket to nearby Asian users
D. Enable S3 Transfer Acceleration on the bucket to improve upload and download speeds for users in Asia through the AWS global edge network
explanation:
Deploy a CloudFront is correct because cloudFront caches at global edge locations, reducing latency for Asian users and lowering S3 transfer costs.
Create an S3 is incorrect because doubles storage costs with replication.
Deploy EC2 instances is incorrect because adds EC2 costs and management overhead.
Enable S3 Transfer is incorrect because improves transfer speed but does not reduce costs or cache content.

question: A media company distributes video content globally through CloudFront. The majority of viewers are in North America and Europe. Data transfer costs from edge locations in South America and Asia Pacific are significantly more expensive. How should they reduce costs?
options: A. Create separate CloudFront distributions for each geographic region and configure different origin servers located in each respective AWS Region
B. Implement geo-restriction to block all viewers from South America and Asia Pacific regions entirely to eliminate the expensive data transfer costs
C. Use Lambda at Edge to redirect users in expensive regions to a lower-quality video stream that requires less bandwidth for delivery to those users
D. Configure the CloudFront distribution to use Price Class 100, which includes only the least expensive North American and European edge locations
explanation:
Configure the CloudFront is correct because price Class 100 limits distribution to cheaper North American and European edges, reducing costs where most viewers are.
Create separate CloudFront is incorrect because degrades experience for some users unnecessarily.
Implement geo-restriction to is incorrect because blocks potential customers entirely, which hurts business revenue.
Use Lambda at is incorrect because adds complexity without reducing edge location pricing.

question: A company uses CloudFront to serve API responses from an ALB origin. Cache hit ratio is only 10 percent because most requests include unique query string parameters. How can they improve the cache hit ratio and reduce origin load?
options: A. Add more origins to the CloudFront distribution using an origin group to distribute the load across multiple ALB endpoints for better performance
B. Disable query string forwarding entirely in the CloudFront cache behavior so all requests to the same path return the same cached response
C. Increase the CloudFront minimum TTL to 24 hours to force longer caching of all responses regardless of the query string variation in requests
D. Configure CloudFront to forward only the query string parameters that affect the API response content to the origin in the cache behavior settings
explanation:
Configure CloudFront to is correct because forwarding only relevant query strings increases cache hits by treating requests with irrelevant parameters as identical.
Add more origins is incorrect because adds origin capacity without improving caching efficiency.
Disable query string is incorrect because caches stale data for queries that do need different responses.
Increase the CloudFront is incorrect because breaks the API if any parameters affect responses.

question: A company serves large software downloads averaging 2 GB each from CloudFront. Downloads spike during product releases. Between releases, traffic is minimal. The company pays a large monthly CloudFront bill. How should they optimize costs?
options: A. Serve downloads directly from S3 during low-traffic periods and switch to CloudFront only during product releases to avoid paying for idle CDN capacity
B. Use CloudFront with Origin Shield enabled to reduce the number of requests reaching S3 and lower both origin and delivery costs during releases
C. Negotiate a CloudFront custom pricing agreement with AWS for a committed traffic volume that provides a lower per-gigabyte data transfer rate
D. Replace CloudFront with S3 Transfer Acceleration for all downloads to leverage edge locations without the ongoing CDN distribution cost overhead
explanation:
Negotiate a CloudFront is correct because custom pricing agreements provide volume discounts for committed traffic, reducing per-GB costs for large file distribution.
Serve downloads directly is incorrect because costs more per GB than CloudFront for downloads.
Use CloudFront with is incorrect because reduces origin costs but not the dominant edge transfer costs.
Replace CloudFront with is incorrect because adds complexity and S3 direct transfer costs more per GB than CloudFront.

question: A news website uses CloudFront to serve articles. Articles are updated every few minutes and the site sets Cache-Control headers to max-age of 60 seconds. The short cache duration means high origin request rates and increased costs. How should they optimize?
options: A. Increase the Cache-Control max-age to 3600 seconds and use CloudFront invalidation API calls each time an article is updated on the origin server
B. Switch from CloudFront to a third-party CDN provider that offers more aggressive caching features and lower per-request pricing for news content
C. Remove CloudFront entirely and serve content directly from the origin to eliminate CDN costs since the short cache TTL provides minimal benefit
D. Enable Origin Shield with a longer TTL on the shield layer to reduce duplicate origin requests from multiple edge locations for the same content
explanation:
Enable Origin Shield is correct because origin Shield adds a centralized cache layer that collapses duplicate requests from edges, reducing origin load with short TTLs.
Increase the Cache-Control is incorrect because increases TTL but invalidation API calls at scale are expensive and slow to propagate.
Switch from CloudFront is incorrect because does not inherently solve the short TTL problem.
Remove CloudFront entirely is incorrect because increases origin costs and latency without CloudFront.

question: A company streams live video to a global audience using CloudFront. Peak concurrent viewers reach 100000. Video is encoded at multiple bitrates. The company wants to minimize delivery costs. Which approach helps the most?
options: A. Use a single high bitrate stream for all viewers to reduce the number of unique cache objects that CloudFront must store at each edge location
B. Implement CloudFront Price Class 100 to serve all global viewers from only the cheapest edge locations in North America and Europe regions
C. Negotiate a CloudFront custom pricing plan based on committed monthly traffic volume to get a reduced per-gigabyte rate for live video delivery
D. Switch from CloudFront to serving the live stream directly from the origin using AWS Elemental MediaPackage without any CDN distribution layer
explanation:
Negotiate a CloudFront is correct because custom pricing with committed volume provides the best per-GB rate for high-volume live streaming.
Use a single is incorrect because without CDN cannot handle 100000 concurrent viewers cost-effectively.
Implement CloudFront Price is incorrect because limits edge locations, degrading experience for viewers outside NA and EU.
Switch from CloudFront is incorrect because increases bandwidth per viewer, raising total costs.

question: A SaaS company uses CloudFront to serve its web application. Static assets like JavaScript and CSS files are versioned with unique filenames. These assets have a Cache-Control max-age of 300 seconds. How should they improve caching efficiency?
options: A. Increase the Cache-Control max-age for versioned static assets to one year since filename versioning ensures updated content gets a new cache key
B. Remove Cache-Control headers entirely and let CloudFront use its default TTL of 24 hours for all static assets served from the application origin
C. Set Cache-Control to no-cache for all static assets to ensure users always receive the most recent version of every JavaScript and CSS file served
D. Implement CloudFront signed URLs for static assets to control who can access cached content and reduce unauthorized requests to the distribution
explanation:
Increase the Cache-Control is correct because versioned filenames make cache invalidation unnecessary, so a one-year max-age maximizes cache hits and reduces origin requests.
Remove Cache-Control headers is incorrect because only extends to 24 hours and loses explicit caching control.
Set Cache-Control to is incorrect because disables caching entirely, increasing origin costs.
Implement CloudFront signed is incorrect because controls access but does not improve caching.

question: A company serves images through CloudFront from an S3 origin. The same image is requested in many different sizes via query string parameters. Each unique combination results in a separate cached object. Cache storage costs are increasing. What should they do?
options: A. Use Lambda at Edge to normalize image size parameters to a fixed set of predefined dimensions so fewer unique cache objects are stored at edges
B. Remove all query string forwarding to cache a single version of each image and handle resizing on the client side using CSS or JavaScript code
C. Create pre-generated images at every possible size and store them as separate S3 objects so CloudFront caches each size without query parameters
D. Disable CloudFront caching for image requests entirely and serve all images directly from S3 to avoid accumulating cached objects at edge locations
explanation:
Use Lambda at is correct because lambda at Edge normalizes requests to a limited set of sizes, dramatically reducing unique cache objects.
Remove all query is incorrect because breaks image sizing functionality.
Create pre-generated images is incorrect because creates enormous numbers of pre-generated images, increasing S3 storage costs.
Disable CloudFront caching is incorrect because eliminates caching benefits entirely, increasing S3 transfer costs.

question: A company uses CloudFront to accelerate its dynamic API. The API returns personalized content, so the cache hit ratio is near zero. The company is paying for CloudFront with almost no caching benefit. Should they keep using CloudFront?
options: A. Remove CloudFront since it provides no caching benefit for personalized content and route API traffic directly to the Application Load Balancer
B. Keep CloudFront because it still reduces latency through persistent connections to the origin and optimized TCP routing across the AWS backbone
C. Replace CloudFront with AWS Global Accelerator since it provides similar network optimization without the overhead of a caching layer for all APIs
D. Configure CloudFront to cache personalized responses using the Authorization header as a cache key to improve the hit ratio for returning users
explanation: Keep CloudFront because cloudFront still reduces latency through connection reuse, SSL offloading, and optimized routing even without caching. Remove CloudFront since is incorrect because caching by Authorization header creates security risks and rarely improves hit ratio for personalized content. Replace CloudFront with is incorrect because provides IP anycast but lacks CloudFront's HTTP optimizations. Configure CloudFront to is incorrect because removes these network benefits.

question: A company distributes firmware updates through CloudFront. Updates are released quarterly and each file is about 100 MB. Between releases, the same files are downloaded millions of times. Transfer costs are the largest expense. What optimization provides the most savings?
options: A. Compress firmware files using gzip before uploading to S3 and configure CloudFront to serve the pre-compressed content to reduce transfer volume
B. Use CloudFront Origin Shield to consolidate origin requests and reduce the number of times the firmware file is fetched from S3 between releases
C. Set Cache-Control max-age to 90 days for firmware files to ensure maximum caching at edge locations and eliminate unnecessary origin fetches
D. Enable S3 Requester Pays on the firmware bucket so that customers downloading updates pay the S3 transfer costs instead of the company paying
explanation:
Set Cache-Control max-age is correct because A 90-day max-age ensures firmware files stay cached for the full quarter between releases, maximizing cache hits.
Compress firmware files is incorrect because helps slightly but firmware binaries may not compress well.
Use CloudFront Origin is incorrect because reduces origin requests but the dominant cost is edge-to-viewer transfer.
Enable S3 Requester is incorrect because does not reduce CloudFront transfer costs and shifts only S3 costs.

question: A global e-commerce company uses CloudFront to serve product pages. The site supports 15 languages, each served from a different URL path prefix. Only 3 languages account for 90 percent of traffic. Edge cache storage across all languages increases costs. How should they optimize?
options: A. Configure CloudFront cache behaviors with longer TTLs for the three popular languages and shorter TTLs for the remaining twelve language paths
B. Serve the three popular languages through CloudFront and serve the remaining twelve languages directly from the origin ALB without any CDN layer
C. Use a single CloudFront distribution with Origin Shield to reduce origin requests for all 15 languages and improve the overall cache efficiency
D. Create separate CloudFront distributions for each language and apply different Price Classes based on where each language audience is located
explanation:
Use a single is correct because origin Shield consolidates cache for all languages, reducing origin requests and improving efficiency.
Configure CloudFront cache is incorrect because adds management complexity for marginal benefit.
Serve the three is incorrect because adds 15 distributions with increased management overhead.
Create separate CloudFront is incorrect because creates two different serving paths and degrades experience for 10 percent of users.

question: A company serves a single-page application from S3 through CloudFront. The application makes API calls to a separate backend. Both CloudFront and the API backend are on separate domains. The company pays for two separate CloudFront distributions. How can they reduce costs?
options: A. Consolidate both the S3 static content and the API backend as two origin groups within a single CloudFront distribution using path-based routing
B. Remove the CloudFront distribution for the S3 static content and serve the SPA directly from an S3 static website hosting endpoint instead
C. Remove the CloudFront distribution for the API backend and have the SPA call the backend ALB directly to eliminate one distribution entirely
D. Merge the SPA and API into a single application running on EC2 behind one ALB as the sole origin for a single CloudFront distribution setup
explanation:
Consolidate both the is correct because A single distribution with path-based routing to S3 and ALB origins consolidates costs and simplifies management.
Remove the CloudFront distribution for the S3 is incorrect because loses CDN benefits for static content.
Remove the CloudFront distribution for the API is incorrect because loses CDN benefits for API and may introduce CORS issues.
Merge the SPA is incorrect because requires merging separate applications, adding complexity.

question: A company uses CloudFront to serve a web application. They notice that 40 percent of requests return 404 Not Found responses, which CloudFront caches and serves. These cached error responses consume edge cache space. How should they handle this?
options: A. Configure a custom error response in CloudFront to return a static error page with a short TTL of 30 seconds to minimize cached error storage time
B. Configure CloudFront to not cache 404 error responses by setting the minimum TTL for error responses to zero in the distribution error pages settings
C. Fix the application to eliminate the 404 errors at the source so that CloudFront never receives or caches Not Found responses from the origin server
D. Increase the TTL for 404 responses so they stay cached longer and reduce the number of requests forwarded to the origin for non-existent resources
explanation:
Fix the application is correct because fixing the source of 404 errors eliminates both unnecessary origin requests and cached error responses.
Configure a custom is incorrect because caches errors longer, which may serve stale responses when resources are later created.
Configure CloudFront to is incorrect because still caches errors briefly.
Increase the TTL is incorrect because sends every 404 request to the origin, increasing origin costs.

question: A company uses CloudFront to deliver content from an S3 origin. They replicated the S3 bucket to a second region for disaster recovery. CloudFront uses only the primary bucket. Both buckets incur storage costs. How can they optimize?
options: A. Configure CloudFront origin failover with an origin group using the primary bucket as primary origin and the replica bucket as secondary origin
B. Remove the replicated bucket and rely on S3 versioning in the primary bucket combined with cross-region replication only for critical objects
C. Replace both S3 buckets with a single origin on EC2 instances that serve content from an EBS volume replicated across Availability Zones only
D. Keep both buckets but enable S3 lifecycle policies on the replica to transition all objects to Glacier Deep Archive to minimize the standby cost
explanation:
Configure CloudFront origin is correct because cloudFront origin failover uses the replica bucket only when the primary fails, providing DR value from the existing replicated bucket.
Remove the replicated is incorrect because reduces DR capability.
Replace both S3 is incorrect because makes replica content inaccessible through CloudFront since Glacier objects cannot be served directly.
Keep both buckets is incorrect because adds EC2 costs and management.

question: A company serves a mobile application backend through CloudFront. The backend returns JSON responses that include user-specific data. The company wants to reduce data transfer costs. Responses average 50 KB each. Which approach helps the most?
options: A. Enable automatic compression in CloudFront to gzip or Brotli compress JSON responses, which typically reduces JSON payload size by 70 to 80 percent
B. Implement response pagination in the API to return smaller JSON payloads per request even though this will increase the total number of requests
C. Cache all JSON responses in CloudFront by using the user ID as a cache key to serve subsequent requests from the same user from the edge cache
D. Move the mobile backend from CloudFront and ALB to API Gateway with Lambda to take advantage of the API Gateway built-in response compression
explanation:
Enable automatic compression is correct because cloudFront automatic compression reduces JSON transfer by 70 to 80 percent, directly cutting data transfer costs.
Implement response pagination is incorrect because caches per-user responses with low hit ratios.
Cache all JSON is incorrect because changes the entire architecture for a feature CloudFront already provides.
Move the mobile is incorrect because increases request count, potentially raising total costs.

question: A company runs a web application on an Auto Scaling group with a minimum of 4, desired of 8, and maximum of 20 instances. Average CPU utilization across the group is 30 percent. The company wants to reduce costs without risking availability. What should they do?
options: A. Reduce the desired capacity from 8 to 4 and set a target tracking scaling policy with a CPU target of 60 percent to scale out when needed
B. Reduce the maximum capacity from 20 to 10 to limit the total number of instances that Auto Scaling can launch during unexpected traffic spikes
C. Change the instance type to a smaller size to right-size individual instances while keeping the same minimum, desired, and maximum capacity values
D. Remove the Auto Scaling group entirely and run a fixed fleet of 4 instances to simplify management and eliminate the Auto Scaling overhead costs
explanation:
Reduce the desired is correct because reducing desired to 4 with target tracking at 60 percent CPU matches current utilization and auto-scales when demand increases.
Reduce the maximum is incorrect because limits maximum but does not reduce current over-provisioning.
Change the instance is incorrect because right-sizes instances but still runs too many.
Remove the Auto is incorrect because removes scaling capability, risking outages during traffic increases.

question: A company uses Auto Scaling with a step scaling policy that adds 2 instances when CPU exceeds 70 percent. During traffic spikes, the group scales out but then scales in too slowly, leaving extra instances running for hours. How should they reduce costs?
options: A. Replace the step scaling policy with a target tracking policy set to 65 percent CPU to enable more responsive scale-in behavior automatically
B. Add a scheduled scaling action to reduce the group size every hour on the hour to force the removal of excess instances during quiet periods
C. Reduce the scale-in cooldown period and lower the CloudWatch alarm threshold for the scale-in action to trigger instance removal more quickly
D. Set the default instance warmup to a very high value so the Auto Scaling group waits longer before considering new instances in scaling decisions
explanation:
Replace the step is correct because target tracking automatically manages both scale-out and scale-in, responding faster to demand changes than step scaling.
Add a scheduled is incorrect because forces arbitrary reductions regardless of actual load.
Reduce the scale-in is incorrect because may cause flapping between scale-out and scale-in.
Set the default is incorrect because delays scaling decisions, which can increase costs.

question: A company runs batch processing on an Auto Scaling group that scales based on an SQS queue depth metric. During peak hours, the group scales to 50 instances. Jobs are fault-tolerant. All instances are On-Demand. How can they reduce costs?
options: A. Configure the Auto Scaling group to use a mixed instances policy with 80 percent Spot and 20 percent On-Demand across multiple instance types
B. Purchase 50 Reserved Instances to cover the peak capacity and keep them running even during off-peak hours to maximize the reservation value
C. Replace the SQS-based scaling with a scheduled scaling policy that launches 50 instances during expected peak hours only each business day
D. Switch from an Auto Scaling group to a fixed fleet of 25 On-Demand instances running around the clock to simplify the scaling configuration
explanation:
Configure the Auto is correct because A mixed instances policy with 80 percent Spot provides massive savings for fault-tolerant batch processing.
Purchase 50 Reserved is incorrect because pays for 25 instances continuously even when the queue is empty.
Replace the SQS-based is incorrect because loses the dynamic scaling based on actual queue depth.
Switch from an is incorrect because over-provisions 50 RIs for variable demand.

question: A company uses Auto Scaling with target tracking at 50 percent CPU for a web application. The group frequently oscillates between 3 and 5 instances every few minutes, causing instability. Instance launches and terminations incur costs. What should they do?
options: A. Increase the target tracking CPU threshold from 50 percent to 70 percent to reduce the sensitivity of the scaling policy to minor fluctuations
B. Increase both the scale-out and scale-in cooldown periods to prevent the Auto Scaling group from reacting too quickly to short traffic variations
C. Remove the Auto Scaling group and deploy a fixed fleet of 4 instances to eliminate the oscillation and provide consistent performance and costs
D. Add a second target tracking policy based on network throughput in addition to CPU to give Auto Scaling more signals for making scaling decisions
explanation:
Increase both the is correct because longer cooldown periods prevent rapid oscillation by giving the group time to stabilize after each scaling action.
Increase the target is incorrect because may lead to over-utilization during real traffic increases.
Remove the Auto is incorrect because adds complexity without addressing the oscillation root cause.
Add a second is incorrect because loses the ability to scale with demand.

question: A company operates an Auto Scaling group across three Availability Zones. The minimum capacity is 6 instances, 2 per AZ. During an AZ failure, the group launches replacement instances in the remaining AZs. The company wants to reduce baseline costs. What should they consider?
options: A. Reduce the minimum capacity to 4 instances and spread them across 3 AZs to lower baseline costs while accepting reduced AZ failure resilience
B. Reduce to 2 Availability Zones and set the minimum to 4 instances to maintain the same capacity per AZ while reducing the baseline instance count
C. Keep 6 instances minimum across 3 AZs but switch to smaller instance types that still handle normal traffic when all AZs are healthy and active
D. Keep 6 instances across 3 AZs but use Spot Instances for 4 of them and reserve only 2 On-Demand instances as the baseline guaranteed capacity
explanation:
Keep 6 instances minimum is correct because right-sizing to smaller instances reduces per-instance cost while maintaining 3 AZ resilience and 6 instance minimum.
Reduce the minimum is incorrect because reduces AZ failure tolerance.
Reduce to 2 is incorrect because loses a third AZ, reducing availability.
Keep 6 instances across is incorrect because puts most of the baseline on Spot, risking multiple simultaneous interruptions.

question: A company runs a web application that experiences predictable traffic peaks every weekday between 9 AM and 5 PM. The Auto Scaling group uses target tracking scaling that reacts to traffic after it arrives, causing brief performance degradation during ramp-up. How should they improve this while controlling costs?
options: A. Increase the maximum capacity of the Auto Scaling group so more instances can be launched quickly when the target tracking policy triggers scaling
B. Add scheduled scaling actions to pre-warm the group before 9 AM and scale down after 5 PM while keeping target tracking for unexpected spikes
C. Replace target tracking with a simple scaling policy that adds more instances per scaling action to respond more aggressively to traffic increases
D. Enable Predictive Scaling to analyze historical traffic patterns and automatically pre-launch instances before the daily traffic peak begins
explanation:
Enable Predictive Scaling is correct because predictive Scaling analyzes patterns and proactively scales before peaks, eliminating ramp-up delays without manual schedule management.
Increase the maximum is incorrect because does not pre-warm instances.
Add scheduled scaling is incorrect because still reacts after traffic arrives.
Replace target tracking is incorrect because works but requires manual schedule maintenance.

question: A company uses Auto Scaling with On-Demand instances for a stateless API. The group scales between 5 and 30 instances daily. The company recently purchased 5 Reserved Instances. How should they configure the group to maximize RI utilization?
options: A. Set the Auto Scaling group minimum and desired capacity to 5 so the Reserved Instances always have running instances to apply their discount to
B. Create a separate fixed fleet of 5 instances for the RIs and a separate Auto Scaling group for additional On-Demand instances beyond the baseline
C. Configure the Auto Scaling group to use only the Reserved Instance type for all scaling to ensure all launched instances receive the RI discount
D. Set the Auto Scaling group maximum to 5 to ensure the group never exceeds the number of Reserved Instances purchased for cost control purposes
explanation:
Set the Auto Scaling group minimum is correct because setting minimum to 5 ensures 5 instances always run and automatically match the RI discount.
Create a separate is incorrect because caps at 5 instances, preventing scaling to meet demand.
Configure the Auto is incorrect because limits instance type flexibility.
Set the Auto Scaling group maximum is incorrect because adds management complexity unnecessarily.

question: A company uses an Auto Scaling group for a microservices application. The application performance depends on available memory, not CPU. The group scales on CPU utilization but often scales too late when memory pressure causes issues. What should they change?
options: A. Create a custom CloudWatch metric for memory utilization published from the instances and use it as the target tracking scaling metric instead
B. Lower the CPU target tracking threshold from 70 percent to 30 percent so scaling happens earlier and indirectly prevents memory pressure issues
C. Increase the instance size to one with more memory to ensure that memory pressure never becomes an issue regardless of the current scaling metric
D. Add an additional scaling policy based on network throughput since high network usage often correlates with increased memory consumption patterns
explanation:
Create a custom is correct because A custom memory metric directly addresses the actual bottleneck for accurate scaling decisions.
Lower the CPU is incorrect because wastes resources by scaling on an irrelevant metric.
Increase the instance is incorrect because uses a proxy metric that may not correlate with memory usage.
Add an additional is incorrect because over-provisions without fixing the scaling signal.

question: A company runs an Auto Scaling group that scales between 10 and 100 instances. They purchase 10 Reserved Instances for the minimum. During scale-out events, all additional instances are On-Demand. The company wants to reduce the cost of the variable capacity. What should they do?
options: A. Purchase 100 Reserved Instances to cover the maximum capacity and ensure every instance benefits from the discounted reservation rate always
B. Add a Compute Savings Plan sized for 100 instances worth of compute to cover all potential scaling capacity at a discounted commitment rate
C. Replace the Auto Scaling group with a fixed fleet of 50 On-Demand instances running continuously to simplify capacity management and billing
D. Configure a mixed instances policy with Spot priority for scale-out instances and On-Demand base capacity matching the 10 Reserved Instances
explanation:
Configure a mixed is correct because mixed instances with Spot for scale-out maximizes savings for the variable portion while RIs cover the base.
Purchase 100 Reserved is incorrect because pays for 50 instances even at low demand.
Add a Compute is incorrect because over-commits Savings Plan for peak capacity that rarely occurs.
Replace the Auto is incorrect because over-commits 100 RIs for variable demand.

question: A company has an Auto Scaling group with a launch template specifying m5.xlarge instances. They want to add Spot Instances but are concerned about capacity availability. What configuration provides the best balance of cost savings and availability?
options: A. Configure the Auto Scaling group to use Spot Instances with a single instance type of m5.xlarge and set the maximum Spot price to the On-Demand rate
B. Create a separate Auto Scaling group using only Spot m5.xlarge instances and manually balance traffic between the two groups using a load balancer
C. Configure a mixed instances policy with multiple instance types including m5.xlarge, m5a.xlarge, m4.xlarge, and m5d.xlarge across all AZs available
D. Replace the Auto Scaling group with a Spot Fleet request that uses the lowest-price allocation strategy for m5.xlarge instances across all zones
explanation:
Configure a mixed is correct because multiple instance types across AZs in a mixed instances policy maximizes Spot capacity pools and reduces interruption risk.
Configure the Auto is incorrect because uses a single Spot pool, increasing interruption risk.
Create a separate is incorrect because requires managing two groups manually.
Replace the Auto is incorrect because uses lowest-price strategy, which is less reliable than capacity-optimized used by mixed instances.

question: A company runs a web application with Auto Scaling. New instances take 5 minutes to warm up before they can handle full traffic. During scale-out events, new instances receive traffic immediately and respond slowly. This wastes capacity and causes poor performance. What should they do?
options: A. Configure a default instance warmup period of 300 seconds so Auto Scaling excludes new instances from scaling metrics until they are fully ready
B. Increase the health check grace period to 600 seconds so that new instances are not terminated prematurely during the warm-up initialization period
C. Add an initial lifecycle hook that pauses new instances in a pending state for five minutes before registering them with the load balancer target group
D. Pre-bake all application dependencies into the AMI so instances start faster and reduce the warm-up time from five minutes to under one minute
explanation:
Add an initial is correct because A lifecycle hook pauses instances until warm-up completes before they receive traffic, preventing wasted capacity.
Configure a default is incorrect because only affects scaling metrics, not traffic routing.
Increase the health is incorrect because prevents early termination but does not prevent early traffic.
Pre-bake all application is incorrect because may reduce but not eliminate warm-up time depending on the application.

question: A company has separate Auto Scaling groups for production, staging, and development, each with identical configurations running m5.xlarge instances. Production runs 24 hours, staging runs 12 hours, and development runs 8 hours per day. How should they optimize costs?
options: A. Consolidate all three environments into a single Auto Scaling group and use tags to differentiate between production, staging, and development
B. Apply scheduled scaling to stop staging and development instances outside their usage hours and use a Savings Plan sized to production only
C. Switch all environments to Spot Instances and implement automatic failover to On-Demand if Spot capacity becomes unavailable during usage hours
D. Purchase Reserved Instances for all three environments based on their maximum running hours to get the deepest discount across every environment
explanation:
Apply scheduled scaling is correct because scheduled scaling for non-production eliminates idle hours and a Savings Plan discounts production.
Consolidate all three is incorrect because merges environments, creating operational risk.
Switch all environments is incorrect because uses Spot for production, which is inappropriate.
Purchase Reserved Instances is incorrect because over-provisions RIs for environments that run only part of the day.

question: A company uses Auto Scaling with target tracking at 40 percent CPU. The group maintains an average of 20 instances. Business analysis shows that the application can tolerate brief CPU spikes up to 80 percent without user impact. How can they reduce costs?
options: A. Increase the target tracking CPU threshold from 40 percent to 70 percent to run fewer instances at higher utilization without impacting user experience
B. Keep the 40 percent threshold but reduce the instance size by half so each instance costs less even though more instances are needed for coverage
C. Remove target tracking and use a fixed desired capacity of 10 instances since the application can handle 80 percent CPU peaks on fewer instances
D. Add a scale-in policy that aggressively removes instances when CPU drops below 20 percent to quickly reduce capacity during every low-traffic period
explanation:
Increase the target is correct because raising the CPU target from 40 to 70 percent reduces the instance count significantly while staying within the 80 percent tolerance.
Keep the 40 is incorrect because may not reduce total cost since more smaller instances are needed.
Remove target tracking is incorrect because causes rapid oscillation with aggressive scale-in.
Add a scale-in is incorrect because removes automatic scaling and risks exceeding 80 percent.

question: A company runs an Auto Scaling group behind an ALB. They use a step scaling policy that adds instances in increments. After a recent traffic surge, the group was slow to scale and response times degraded. The company wants faster scaling that is also cost-effective. What should they do?
options: A. Switch from step scaling to target tracking scaling which continuously adjusts the group size and reacts more smoothly to changing traffic loads
B. Reduce the CloudWatch alarm evaluation period from 5 minutes to 1 minute so the step scaling policy detects traffic increases more quickly overall
C. Enable Predictive Scaling alongside the existing step scaling policy so the group pre-launches instances based on anticipated traffic pattern analysis
D. Increase the step adjustment to add 10 instances at each step instead of 2 to ensure the group has enough capacity immediately during every surge
explanation:
Enable Predictive Scaling is correct because predictive Scaling proactively launches instances before surges based on patterns, complementing reactive step scaling.
Switch from step is incorrect because detects faster but step scaling still adds instances slowly in increments.
Reduce the CloudWatch is incorrect because over-provisions on every scaling event, wasting money.
Increase the step is incorrect because improves reactivity but still responds after traffic arrives.

question: A company manages an Auto Scaling group with instances in a single Availability Zone to minimize cross-AZ data transfer costs. The group has a minimum of 5 instances. A recent AZ outage caused a complete application outage. How should they balance cost and availability?
options: A. Expand the Auto Scaling group to three Availability Zones and accept the cross-AZ data transfer costs as the necessary price of high availability
B. Keep the single AZ configuration but add a standby Auto Scaling group in a second AZ that launches instances only when the primary AZ is unhealthy
C. Expand to two Availability Zones and use VPC endpoints and proper architecture to minimize cross-AZ data transfer while improving availability
D. Keep the single AZ and implement faster instance recovery using Auto Scaling health checks to replace failed instances within the same AZ quickly
explanation:
Expand to two is correct because two AZs with minimized cross-AZ transfer provides a cost-effective balance between availability and data transfer costs.
Expand the Auto is incorrect because adds maximum transfer costs across three AZs.
Keep the single AZ configuration is incorrect because adds complexity with standby groups and slower failover.
Keep the single AZ and is incorrect because does not protect against a full AZ outage.

question: A company spends 50000 dollars per month on EC2 On-Demand instances across multiple instance families and regions. Workloads are stable and expected to continue for at least a year. The company wants maximum flexibility with meaningful savings. Which Savings Plan type should they choose?
options: A. Purchase a one-year Compute Savings Plan with No Upfront payment to get flexibility across instance families, regions, and compute services
B. Purchase one-year EC2 Instance Savings Plans for the specific instance families used today to get a deeper discount than Compute Savings Plans
C. Purchase three-year Standard Reserved Instances for all current instances to get the deepest possible discount for the one-year expected duration
D. Continue using On-Demand pricing and apply for the AWS Free Tier to reduce costs since the workloads may change after the one-year period ends
explanation:
Purchase a one-year is correct because A Compute Savings Plan provides flexibility across families and regions with significant savings for a stable one-year commitment.
Purchase one-year EC2 is incorrect because offers deeper savings but locks into specific families, reducing flexibility.
Purchase three-year Standard is incorrect because commits for three years when the need is only one year.
Continue using On-Demand is incorrect because provides no savings for this spending level.

question: A company uses both EC2 instances and Lambda functions extensively. They want a single commitment that covers both services. Which option provides this capability?
options: A. Purchase EC2 Instance Savings Plans which automatically apply discounts to both EC2 instances and Lambda functions in the same AWS account
B. Purchase Compute Savings Plans which apply to EC2 instances, Lambda functions, and Fargate tasks across all regions in the account automatically
C. Purchase Standard Reserved Instances which provide discounts across all compute services including EC2, Lambda, and Fargate usage together
D. Purchase a combination of EC2 Instance Savings Plans and separate Lambda Reserved Concurrency allocations to cover both services independently
explanation:
Purchase Compute Savings is correct because compute Savings Plans uniquely cover EC2, Lambda, and Fargate under a single commitment.
Purchase EC2 Instance is incorrect because with EC2 Instance Savings Plans only covers EC2.
Purchase Standard Reserved is incorrect because with Standard RIs only covers EC2 instances.
Purchase a combination is incorrect because does not exist since there are no Lambda Reserved Concurrency purchase plans.

question: A company runs workloads across m5, c5, and r5 instance families. They plan to migrate to Graviton-based m6g, c6g, and r6g instances over the next year. Which Savings Plan allows them to change instance families without losing their discount?
options: A. Purchase EC2 Instance Savings Plans for m5, c5, and r5 families and exchange them for m6g, c6g, and r6g plans after migration completes
B. Purchase Standard Reserved Instances for current instances and sell them on the Reserved Instance Marketplace before migrating to Graviton types
C. Purchase Convertible Reserved Instances for the current instance families and convert them to Graviton families when the migration is complete
D. Purchase Compute Savings Plans which automatically apply to any instance family, size, OS, or tenancy without requiring changes or exchanges
explanation:
Purchase Compute Savings is correct because compute Savings Plans automatically apply across any instance family, making them ideal for planned migrations.
Purchase EC2 Instance is incorrect because requires separate plans per family, which cannot be exchanged.
Purchase Standard Reserved is incorrect because requires selling RIs, which is complex and may result in losses.
Purchase Convertible Reserved is incorrect because works but Convertible RIs provide less savings than Compute Savings Plans.

question: A company has a steady baseline of 10 m5.xlarge instances running 24 hours a day in us-east-1. They also have variable workloads across other instance types and regions. What combination of Savings Plans maximizes savings?
options: A. Purchase a single Compute Savings Plan sized to cover the total average monthly spend across all instances, regions, and instance types combined
B. Purchase Standard Reserved Instances for the 10 m5.xlarge baseline and use On-Demand pricing for all variable workloads without any Savings Plans
C. Purchase Compute Savings Plans for everything to maintain maximum flexibility across all instance types, sizes, regions, and operating systems used
D. Purchase EC2 Instance Savings Plans for the 10 m5 baseline in us-east-1 and a Compute Savings Plan for the remaining variable spend across regions
explanation:
Purchase EC2 Instance is correct because EC2 Instance Savings Plans give deeper discounts for the predictable m5 baseline, while Compute Plans cover variable workloads flexibly.
Purchase a single is incorrect because uses only Compute Plans, missing deeper EC2 Instance Plan discounts for the stable baseline.
Purchase Standard Reserved is incorrect because also misses the deeper discount opportunity.
Purchase Compute Savings is incorrect because uses RIs without Savings Plans for the variable portion.

question: A startup is growing rapidly and expects compute usage to triple over the next year. They want to save on current spending without over-committing. What Savings Plan strategy should they use?
options: A. Purchase a three-year Compute Savings Plan sized to the expected tripled compute level to lock in savings for the full growth trajectory now
B. Purchase a one-year Compute Savings Plan sized to the current compute baseline and purchase additional plans as usage grows over the year
C. Avoid Savings Plans entirely and use only On-Demand instances until growth stabilizes so the company does not over-commit during rapid expansion
D. Purchase EC2 Instance Savings Plans for triple the current usage to guarantee capacity and savings for the anticipated growth in compute demand
explanation:
Purchase a one-year is correct because starting with a plan sized to current baseline and adding more as growth materializes avoids over-commitment.
Purchase a three-year is incorrect because commits to a tripled level that may not materialize as expected.
Avoid Savings Plans is incorrect because misses savings on current stable spending.
Purchase EC2 Instance is incorrect because over-commits to a specific instance family at triple the current usage.

question: A company has a one-year Compute Savings Plan that covers 80 percent of their EC2 spend. They are evaluating whether to purchase an additional plan. On-Demand spend averages 5000 dollars per month for the remaining 20 percent. What should they do?
options: A. Purchase an additional one-year Compute Savings Plan sized to cover the remaining 5000 dollars per month of On-Demand spend for full coverage
B. Do nothing because the remaining 20 percent On-Demand spend provides flexibility for workload changes without additional commitment obligations
C. Replace the existing Compute Savings Plan with an EC2 Instance Savings Plan to get a deeper discount that might cover the remaining 20 percent
D. Purchase an additional one-year EC2 Instance Savings Plan sized to the specific instance types making up the remaining On-Demand spend for savings
explanation:
Purchase an additional one-year EC2 is correct because an EC2 Instance Savings Plan for the specific remaining instances provides a deeper discount than another Compute Plan.
Purchase an additional one-year Compute is incorrect because cannot replace an active Savings Plan mid-term.
Replace the existing is incorrect because works but gives less discount than an EC2 Instance Plan for known workloads.

question: A company runs EC2 instances primarily in us-east-1 but occasionally bursts into us-west-2 and eu-west-1. They want a Savings Plan that applies across all regions. Which plan should they choose?
options: A. Purchase EC2 Instance Savings Plans in us-east-1 which automatically apply to the same instance families running in any other AWS Region as well
B. Purchase separate EC2 Instance Savings Plans in each of the three regions to ensure coverage for instances running in us-east-1, us-west-2, and eu-west-1
C. Purchase a Compute Savings Plan which automatically applies to EC2 usage in any region without requiring region-specific plan purchases or configuration
D. Purchase Standard Reserved Instances with regional scope in us-east-1 and convert them to apply across other regions using the modification feature
explanation:
Purchase a Compute is correct because compute Savings Plans apply across all regions automatically.
Purchase EC2 Instance is incorrect because it does not meet the specific requirements of this scenario.
Purchase separate EC2 is incorrect because requires purchasing three separate plans.
Purchase Standard Reserved is incorrect because cannot convert regional RIs to cross-region scope.

question: A company purchased a three-year Compute Savings Plan a year ago. Their compute usage has decreased significantly. The Savings Plan commitment now exceeds actual usage. What can they do?
options: A. Sell the unused portion of the Savings Plan on the AWS Marketplace to recover some of the committed spend that is no longer needed for operations
B. Nothing can be done because Savings Plans cannot be sold, exchanged, or canceled once purchased, so the commitment must be honored until expiry
C. Convert the three-year Compute Savings Plan to a one-year plan with lower commitment to match the reduced compute usage going forward from today
D. Contact AWS Support to cancel the Savings Plan early and receive a prorated refund for the remaining two years of the unused commitment period
explanation:
Nothing can be is correct because savings Plans are non-cancellable and non-transferable commitments that must be honored through their full term.
Sell the unused is incorrect because is wrong because Savings Plans cannot be sold on the marketplace unlike RIs.
Convert the three-year is incorrect because is wrong because Savings Plans cannot be converted or modified.
Contact AWS Support is incorrect because is wrong because AWS does not offer early cancellation or refunds.

question: A company is deciding between All Upfront, Partial Upfront, and No Upfront payment options for a one-year Compute Savings Plan. The company has limited cash reserves. Which option provides the best balance of savings and cash flow?
options: A. Choose All Upfront payment to get the maximum discount even though it requires the full commitment amount to be paid at the time of purchase immediately
B. Choose Partial Upfront payment to get a moderate discount while splitting the cost between an upfront payment and reduced monthly installments
C. Choose No Upfront payment to preserve cash flow and still receive a meaningful discount compared to On-Demand pricing with no initial expenditure
D. Skip the Savings Plan entirely and continue paying On-Demand rates since the limited cash reserves make any upfront commitment financially risky
explanation:
Choose No Upfront is correct because no Upfront preserves cash while still providing significant savings over On-Demand pricing.
Choose All Upfront is incorrect because strains limited cash reserves with full upfront payment.
Choose Partial Upfront is incorrect because still requires a significant upfront cash outlay.
Skip the Savings is incorrect because misses all savings opportunities.

question: A company runs workloads on both EC2 and Fargate. EC2 accounts for 70 percent of compute spend and Fargate for 30 percent. They want one plan to cover both. How should they size their Compute Savings Plan?
options: A. Size the plan to cover 100 percent of the combined EC2 and Fargate hourly spend to maximize the discount applied across both compute services
B. Size the plan to cover only the EC2 portion since Compute Savings Plans do not apply to Fargate tasks running on either capacity provider type
C. Size the plan to cover approximately 70 to 80 percent of total compute spend to get savings while keeping flexibility for workload fluctuations
D. Purchase separate Compute Savings Plans for EC2 and Fargate since the discount rates are different and require independent commitment calculations
explanation:
Size the plan to cover approximately is correct because covering 70 to 80 percent leaves room for variability while maximizing committed savings.
Size the plan to cover 100 is incorrect because incorrectly states that Fargate is not covered by Compute Plans.
Size the plan to cover only is incorrect because is unnecessary since one plan covers both services.
Purchase separate Compute is incorrect because risks over-committing if usage fluctuates.

question: A company uses EC2 instances in a single region with c5.xlarge instances only. They need the deepest possible discount. Which purchasing option provides the maximum savings?
options: A. Purchase a three-year Compute Savings Plan with All Upfront payment for the maximum discount available across all Savings Plan types and terms
B. Purchase a three-year EC2 Instance Savings Plan with All Upfront payment for c5 family in the specific region to get the deepest plan discount
C. Purchase three-year Standard Reserved Instances with All Upfront payment for c5.xlarge in the specific region to get the absolute deepest discount
D. Purchase a one-year EC2 Instance Savings Plan with All Upfront payment and renew it annually to avoid the risk of a three-year term commitment
explanation:
Purchase three-year Standard is correct because three-year Standard RIs with All Upfront for a specific instance type in a single region provide the absolute deepest discount.
Purchase a three-year Compute is incorrect because compute Plans offer less savings than EC2 Instance Plans for a single family.
Purchase a three-year EC2 is incorrect because with one-year terms provides less savings than three-year.
Purchase a one-year is incorrect because offers deep savings but Standard RIs still provide slightly deeper discounts.

question: A company has 200 instances across 10 AWS accounts in an AWS Organization. They want to share Savings Plan benefits across all accounts. How should they configure this?
options: A. Purchase separate Savings Plans in each of the 10 accounts to ensure each account receives its own dedicated discount for the instances it operates
B. Purchase Savings Plans in the management account and enable discount sharing in AWS Organizations to apply benefits across all member accounts
C. Purchase Savings Plans in any member account because Organizations automatically shares all Savings Plan benefits across all linked accounts always
D. Contact AWS Support to manually configure cross-account Savings Plan sharing since this feature requires special activation for each Organization
explanation:
Purchase Savings Plans in the is correct because purchasing in the management account with discount sharing enabled distributes benefits across all member accounts.
Purchase separate Savings is incorrect because works but management account is the best practice.
Purchase Savings Plans in any is incorrect because is more expensive due to fragmented commitments.
Contact AWS Support is incorrect because is incorrect since sharing is a self-service Organizations feature.

question: A company is comparing EC2 Instance Savings Plans to Standard Reserved Instances. Both offer similar discounts. What advantage do EC2 Instance Savings Plans have over Standard RIs?
options: A. EC2 Instance Savings Plans can be applied across different operating systems and tenancies within the same instance family without modifications
B. EC2 Instance Savings Plans provide deeper discounts than Standard Reserved Instances for the same commitment term and payment option combination
C. EC2 Instance Savings Plans can be sold on the Reserved Instance Marketplace if the company no longer needs the committed capacity for workloads
D. EC2 Instance Savings Plans automatically apply to Lambda and Fargate usage in addition to EC2 instances across all supported compute services
explanation:
EC2 Instance Savings Plans can be applied is correct because EC2 Instance Savings Plans offer size, OS, and tenancy flexibility within a family, while Standard RIs lock to specific attributes.
EC2 Instance Savings Plans provide is incorrect because describes Compute Savings Plans, not EC2 Instance Plans.
EC2 Instance Savings Plans can be sold is incorrect because is wrong since Savings Plans cannot be sold on the marketplace.
EC2 Instance Savings Plans automatically is incorrect because is incorrect as discounts are similar.

question: A company purchased one-year EC2 Instance Savings Plans six months ago. They now realize they need to migrate from Linux to Windows instances in the same family. How does this affect their Savings Plans?
options: A. They need to purchase new EC2 Instance Savings Plans for Windows because existing Linux plans do not apply to Windows instance operating systems
B. The existing EC2 Instance Savings Plans automatically apply to Windows instances in the same family since plans are flexible across operating systems
C. They must contact AWS Support to modify the existing plans from Linux to Windows coverage before the migration can benefit from plan discounts
D. They need to exchange the Linux EC2 Instance Savings Plans for Windows plans using the Savings Plan exchange feature in the AWS Billing console
explanation:
The existing EC2 is correct because EC2 Instance Savings Plans are flexible across OS, tenancy, and size within the same family and region.
They need to purchase is incorrect because is wrong because there is no exchange feature for Savings Plans.
They must contact is incorrect because incorrectly states they are OS-specific.
They need to exchange is incorrect because is unnecessary since flexibility is automatic.

question: A company wants to track how much of their Savings Plans commitment is being utilized versus wasted. Which AWS tool provides this visibility?
options: A. AWS Cost Explorer Savings Plans utilization and coverage reports that show how effectively purchased Savings Plans are being applied to usage
B. AWS Trusted Advisor Savings Plans checks that monitor utilization rates and send alerts when Savings Plans are not being fully consumed daily
C. Amazon CloudWatch Savings Plans metrics dashboard that displays real-time utilization of all active Savings Plans in the current AWS account
D. AWS Budgets Savings Plans tracking that automatically creates budget alerts when Savings Plan utilization drops below a configured threshold value
explanation:
AWS Cost Explorer is correct because cost Explorer provides detailed Savings Plans utilization and coverage reports showing exactly where plans are applied.
AWS Trusted Advisor is incorrect because is limited in Savings Plan analysis.
Amazon CloudWatch Savings is incorrect because does not exist as a CloudWatch feature.
AWS Budgets Savings is incorrect because can alert on budgets but does not provide detailed utilization reporting.

question: A company runs a web scraping application that collects data from thousands of websites. Each scraping task takes about 10 minutes and is completely independent. The application currently runs on On-Demand instances. How should they reduce costs?
options: A. Switch to Spot Instances with a diversified fleet across multiple instance types and Availability Zones using capacity-optimized allocation strategy
B. Purchase one-year Reserved Instances for the scraping fleet to reduce the per-hour cost while maintaining the same On-Demand instance reliability
C. Migrate the scraping application to Lambda functions triggered by an SQS queue to eliminate EC2 costs and pay only for actual scraping execution time
D. Switch to a single large Spot Instance type to consolidate all scraping tasks and reduce the number of instances that need to be managed daily
explanation:
Switch to Spot is correct because diversified Spot fleet with capacity-optimized allocation provides the lowest cost with best availability for independent tasks.
Purchase one-year Reserved is incorrect because commits to RIs for a variable workload.
Migrate the scraping is incorrect because may hit Lambda networking limits for web scraping.
Switch to a is incorrect because uses a single Spot pool, increasing interruption risk.

question: A machine learning team trains models using GPU instances. Training jobs take 2 to 8 hours and can checkpoint progress every 30 minutes. The team wants to use Spot Instances but is concerned about losing training progress. What should they implement?
options: A. Use Spot Instances with checkpointing to S3 every 30 minutes and configure the training script to resume from the latest checkpoint on new instances
B. Use On-Demand GPU instances to avoid any interruption risk and ensure training jobs always complete without losing progress or requiring restarts
C. Use Spot Instances without checkpointing and simply restart training from the beginning whenever a Spot interruption terminates the training instance
D. Use Spot Instances with a maximum price set to the On-Demand rate to prevent interruptions by ensuring the bid always exceeds the market price
explanation:
Use Spot Instances with checkpointing is correct because checkpointing every 30 minutes to S3 limits maximum progress loss and enables seamless restart on new Spot Instances.
Use On-Demand GPU is incorrect because risks losing hours of training progress on each interruption.
Use Spot Instances without is incorrect because does not prevent interruptions since Spot reclamation is based on capacity, not price.
Use Spot Instances with a is incorrect because uses expensive On-Demand pricing.

question: A video encoding company runs transcoding jobs on c5.2xlarge Spot Instances. They experience frequent interruptions in us-east-1a. Jobs must be completed within a deadline. How should they improve Spot availability while controlling costs?
options: A. Switch to On-Demand c5.2xlarge instances to guarantee availability and ensure all transcoding jobs meet their deadlines without interruption
B. Use a Spot Fleet with capacity-optimized allocation across c5.2xlarge, c5a.2xlarge, c5n.2xlarge, and c4.2xlarge in all available Availability Zones
C. Set the maximum Spot price to three times the current Spot price to reduce the frequency of interruptions for the c5.2xlarge instance type used
D. Switch to a single Spot pool in us-east-1b instead of us-east-1a since different Availability Zones have different Spot availability characteristics
explanation:
Use a Spot is correct because A diversified Spot Fleet across multiple types and AZs maximizes capacity pool other optionsnd reduces interruption risk.
Switch to On-Demand is incorrect because eliminates savings.
Set the maximum is incorrect because does not reduce interruptions since they are capacity-based, not price-based.
Switch to a is incorrect because still relies on a single pool, which can also experience interruptions.

question: A company uses Spot Instances for a batch data processing pipeline. When Spot Instances are interrupted, jobs are re-queued in SQS and retried. However, some jobs have been retried more than 10 times, significantly delaying completion. How should they address this?
options: A. Set a maximum retry count in SQS and move jobs that exceed it to a dead-letter queue for processing on On-Demand instances as a fallback mechanism
B. Switch all batch processing to On-Demand instances to eliminate interruptions and ensure every job completes on the first attempt without any retries
C. Increase the SQS visibility timeout to 24 hours so that interrupted jobs have more time to be picked up by a new Spot Instance before becoming visible
D. Use Spot Instance hibernation to pause interrupted instances instead of terminating them so jobs resume automatically when capacity becomes available
explanation:
Set a maximum is correct because A dead-letter queue with On-Demand fallback ensures persistently failing jobs complete while keeping Spot for most jobs.
Switch all batch is incorrect because abandons all Spot savings.
Increase the SQS is incorrect because does not address the repeated interruption issue.
Use Spot Instance is incorrect because is limited to specific instance types and may not be available for the instances used.

question: A game development studio runs nightly build and test automation on a fleet of 20 Spot Instances. Builds take about one hour. On some nights, Spot capacity is insufficient and builds do not complete. How should they ensure build completion at the lowest cost?
options: A. Replace all 20 Spot Instances with On-Demand instances for the nightly build to guarantee capacity and ensure builds complete every single night
B. Use an Auto Scaling group with a mixed instances policy specifying 80 percent Spot and 20 percent On-Demand capacity across multiple instance types
C. Reserve 20 instances using On-Demand Capacity Reservations for the nightly build window and release them after the build completes each morning
D. Switch to a Spot Fleet with a lowest-price allocation strategy using only one instance type to get the cheapest possible price for the build fleet
explanation:
Use an Auto is correct because mixed instances with 80 percent Spot and 20 percent On-Demand ensures builds complete while maximizing savings.
Replace all 20 is incorrect because uses all On-Demand, missing Spot savings.
Reserve 20 instances is incorrect because reserves capacity at On-Demand rates with no discount.
Switch to a is incorrect because with a single type increases interruption risk and does not guarantee availability.

question: A data analytics company processes large datasets using Spark on EMR with Spot Instances for task nodes. During processing, Spot interruptions cause task failures and job restarts. How should they configure EMR to handle this efficiently?
options: A. Use On-Demand instances for all EMR nodes including task nodes to avoid any interruptions that could cause expensive Spark job restarts and delays
B. Use Spot for task nodes with instance fleet configuration across multiple types and enable Spark speculation to rerun slow tasks on available nodes
C. Use Spot Instances only for the EMR core nodes and On-Demand for task nodes since core nodes store HDFS data that must not be interrupted at all
D. Increase the number of Spot task nodes by three times to ensure enough spare capacity exists to absorb interruptions without affecting job progress
explanation:
Use Spot for is correct because instance fleet with multiple types maximizes Spot availability and Spark speculation handles task failures gracefully.
Use On-Demand instances is incorrect because puts HDFS data on interruptible Spot core nodes, which is dangerous.
Use Spot Instances is incorrect because eliminates savings.
Increase the number is incorrect because wastes money on excess capacity.

question: A company wants to run a stateless web application on Spot Instances behind an ALB. The application must maintain at least 4 instances at all times for availability. How should they configure the infrastructure?
options: A. Create an Auto Scaling group with minimum of 4 On-Demand instances and no Spot to ensure the web application always has guaranteed base capacity
B. Create an Auto Scaling group with mixed instances policy, On-Demand base of 4 instances, and Spot for any additional capacity above the baseline
C. Create two separate Auto Scaling groups, one with 4 On-Demand instances and another with Spot Instances, both behind the same Application LB
D. Create an Auto Scaling group with 8 Spot Instances minimum to ensure at least 4 survive any interruption event at any given time during operation
explanation:
Create an Auto Scaling group with mixed is correct because mixed instances with On-Demand base of 4 guarantees minimum capacity while using Spot for cost-effective scaling above baseline.
Create an Auto Scaling group with minimum is incorrect because uses no Spot, missing savings on scale-out.
Create two separate is incorrect because requires managing two groups.
Create an Auto Scaling group with 8 is incorrect because over-provisions Spot without guaranteeing 4 will always be available.

question: A company runs containerized batch jobs on ECS with EC2 Spot Instances as the capacity provider. Some containers hold state in memory and lose significant progress when Spot Instances are interrupted. How can they mitigate this?
options: A. Monitor the EC2 Spot Instance interruption notice and trigger a container checkpoint to save state to EFS before the two-minute termination window
B. Switch all ECS capacity to On-Demand instances to eliminate the interruption risk and ensure all containers complete without losing in-memory state
C. Increase container memory allocation so containers process batches faster and reduce the window of vulnerability to Spot interruption events overall
D. Use ECS Fargate instead of EC2 capacity to avoid Spot interruptions entirely since Fargate manages the underlying infrastructure automatically
explanation:
Monitor the EC2 is correct because monitoring the two-minute warning and checkpointing to EFS preserves progress while maintaining Spot savings.
Switch all ECS is incorrect because eliminates Spot savings.
Increase container memory is incorrect because does not prevent data loss during interruptions.
Use ECS Fargate is incorrect because with standard Fargate costs more and Fargate Spot also has interruptions.

question: A genomics company runs analysis pipelines that create hundreds of intermediate files during processing. When Spot Instances are interrupted, intermediate files on instance storage are lost and the entire pipeline must restart. How should they reduce the blast radius?
options: A. Store all intermediate files on Amazon EFS mounted to the Spot Instances so that data persists independently of instance lifecycle and interruptions
B. Use larger Spot Instances so the pipeline runs faster and the probability of interruption during the shorter processing window is statistically lower
C. Switch to On-Demand instances to avoid interruptions entirely and ensure intermediate files on instance storage are never lost during processing
D. Write intermediate files to local SSD storage which is faster than network storage and accept the risk of occasional data loss from interruptions
explanation:
Store all intermediate is correct because EFS persists intermediate files across Spot interruptions, enabling pipeline resumption from the last stage.
Use larger Spot is incorrect because abandons Spot savings.
Switch to On-Demand is incorrect because reduces but does not eliminate risk.
Write intermediate files is incorrect because explicitly accepts data loss, which does not reduce the blast radius.

question: A rendering farm uses GPU Spot Instances for 3D rendering. Each frame takes about 15 minutes. The company wants to minimize the cost per frame while handling interruptions. Frames can be re-rendered without penalty. What architecture should they use?
options: A. Use Spot Instances with a maximum price set to 50 percent of On-Demand to limit spending but accept that instances may be interrupted more often
B. Use a single p3.16xlarge On-Demand instance to render many frames in parallel on a single reliable host to avoid the overhead of Spot management
C. Use a Spot Fleet with capacity-optimized allocation across multiple GPU types and an SQS queue to track frames and reassign interrupted frames
D. Use Reserved GPU instances to render frames at a predictable cost per hour and avoid any risk of interruption during the 15-minute render window
explanation:
Use a Spot is correct because A diversified Spot Fleet with SQS provides the lowest cost per frame with automatic re-rendering of interrupted frames.
Use Spot Instances is incorrect because limits the maximum Spot price, reducing availability without cost benefit.
Use a single is incorrect because uses expensive Reserved Instances for variable rendering demand.
Use Reserved GPU is incorrect because uses expensive On-Demand with no savings.

question: A company receives a two-minute Spot Instance interruption notice. The instance runs a long-running data export job. Two minutes is not enough time to complete the export. What should they implement to handle this gracefully?
options: A. Configure the instance to automatically save a progress checkpoint to DynamoDB when the interruption notice is received so the job can resume later
B. Set the Spot Instance interruption behavior to hibernate so the instance state is preserved to EBS and the job can continue when capacity is available
C. Ignore the interruption notice since Spot Instances are not always actually interrupted after the two-minute notice is issued by the EC2 service
D. Request a Spot Instance with a defined duration block of six hours to guarantee uninterrupted capacity for the full duration of the export job run
explanation:
Configure the instance is correct because saving a checkpoint to DynamoDB on interruption notice enables the job to resume from where it stopped on a new instance.
Set the Spot is incorrect because requires hibernation support, which is not available for all instance types and volumes.
Ignore the interruption is incorrect because risks data loss since interruptions usually proceed.
Request a Spot is incorrect because with defined duration blocks is no longer available for new requests.

question: A company uses Spot Instances in an Auto Scaling group. They notice that their Spot Instances are frequently from a single capacity pool, increasing the risk of simultaneous interruptions. How should they improve diversity?
options: A. Configure the Auto Scaling group mixed instances policy with at least six different instance types and use capacity-optimized-prioritized allocation
B. Set the Auto Scaling group to use a single large instance type to reduce the number of instances needed and therefore reduce interruption probability
C. Configure the mixed instances policy with only two instance types to keep configuration simple while still providing some diversification benefit
D. Use a Spot Fleet instead of an Auto Scaling group because Spot Fleets automatically diversify across more capacity pools than Auto Scaling groups do
explanation:
Configure the Auto is correct because six or more instance types with capacity-optimized allocation maximizes pool diversity and minimizes correlated interruptions.
Set the Auto is incorrect because does not inherently provide more diversity than a well-configured Auto Scaling group.
Configure the mixed is incorrect because concentrates on one pool, increasing risk.
Use a Spot is incorrect because provides minimal diversity.

question: A company runs a CI/CD pipeline with Spot Instances for building and testing. Build agents pull source code from CodeCommit, compile, run tests, and push artifacts to S3. Spot interruptions cause build failures. What is the most practical mitigation?
options: A. Switch the CI/CD pipeline to use AWS CodeBuild which manages compute automatically and does not use Spot Instances that could be interrupted
B. Configure build agents to save incremental compilation state to S3 and restore it when a build restarts on a new Spot Instance after interruption
C. Run builds on On-Demand instances to guarantee completion and use Spot only for running the test suites since tests can be restarted independently
D. Set up a Jenkins controller on an On-Demand instance that automatically retries the entire build pipeline when a Spot agent is interrupted midway
explanation:
Set up a is correct because auto-retrying the full build from a Jenkins controller is practical since builds are idempotent and typically short.
Switch the CI/CD is incorrect because moves to CodeBuild, which costs more than Spot for heavy CI/CD usage.
Configure build agents is incorrect because adds complexity for incremental state management.
Run builds on is incorrect because splits the pipeline unnecessarily when full retries are simpler.

question: A company operates a real-time data ingestion service that cannot tolerate any downtime. They want to use Spot Instances to reduce costs. Is this feasible, and if so, how?
options: A. Spot Instances are not suitable for real-time services that cannot tolerate downtime because interruptions would cause unacceptable service disruption
B. Use Spot Instances with Spot Instance hibernation enabled so the service automatically resumes within seconds when capacity becomes available again
C. Use Spot Instances exclusively with a very high maximum price set to the On-Demand rate to prevent interruptions from ever occurring during operation
D. Use a mixed architecture with On-Demand instances for the minimum required capacity and Spot Instances for additional throughput during peak periods
explanation:
Use a mixed is correct because A mixed architecture uses On-Demand for guaranteed baseline and Spot for cost-effective burst capacity without risking core availability.
Spot Instances are is incorrect because incorrectly says Spot is never suitable when a mixed approach works.
Use Spot Instances with is incorrect because hibernation has startup delays that violate zero-downtime requirements.
Use Spot Instances exclusively is incorrect because does not prevent interruptions since they are capacity-based.

question: A company uses Spot Instances for a big data processing cluster. They want to understand and plan for Spot pricing and interruption patterns. Which AWS tool provides this information?
options: A. AWS Cost Explorer which shows historical Spot Instance spending trends and allows forecasting of future Spot costs based on past usage patterns
B. The Spot Instance pricing history in the EC2 console or API which shows price changes and the Spot placement score for capacity availability
C. AWS Trusted Advisor which provides recommendations on Spot Instance usage and alerts when Spot prices exceed On-Demand rates in a given region
D. Amazon CloudWatch Spot metrics dashboard which displays real-time Spot pricing, interruption frequency, and capacity pool availability metrics
explanation:
